titleEn: The Mu'allaqa of Antara

verses.ajuz: أَمْ هَلْ عَرَفْتَ الدَّارَ بَعْدَ تَوَهُّمِ

translationEn: Have the poets left anything to be patched? Or did you recognize the dwelling after long uncertainty? O dwelling of Abla at al-Jiwa, speak to me! Good morning, dwelling of Abla, and be safe! I halted my she-camel there, huge as a palace, to fulfill the need of a lingering lover. Abla alights at al-Jiwa while our people are at al-Hazn, then al-Samman, then al-Mutathallam. If you have resolved upon departure, then your riding camels were bridled in a dark night. Nothing alarmed me but the pack-beasts of her people in the midst of the dwellings, munching khimkhim seeds. Among them were forty-two milch camels, black as the wing-feathers of a jet-black crow. You have settled — think nothing else of it — in my heart in the station of a beloved held in highest honor.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: something to patch or mend (a theme)

context: The opening of Antara's Mu'allaqa, which begins with the bold rhetorical question "Have the poets left anything to be patched?" — challenging the convention of the desert prelude while simultaneously invoking it. The poem then turns to his love for Abla, his cousin whom he was forbidden to marry due to his mixed heritage.

titleEn: Love for Abla

verses.ajuz: مِنِّي وَبِيضُ الهِنْدِ تَقْطُرُ مِنْ دَمِي

verses.sadr: يَا عَبْلَ قَدْ هَامَ الفُؤَادُ بِحُبِّكِ

translationEn: I remembered you while spears were drinking their fill from me and the white blades of Indian swords dripped with my blood. I wished to kiss the swords because they gleamed like the flash of your smiling teeth. O Abla, my heart wanders in love for you — by the right of your love, do not blame Antara! If you ask about that, Abla will tell you that I am foremost in sharing the spoils of war.
context: Among Antara's most famous verses, merging the battlefield with declarations of love. The image of wishing to kiss the swords because they resemble Abla's gleaming smile became one of the most celebrated conceits in Arabic poetry.

titleEn: Boast of Valor

verses.sadr: وَلَقَدْ خَشِيتُ بِأَنْ أَمُوتَ وَلَمْ تَدُرْ
إِذْ يَتَّقُونَ بِيَ الأَسِنَّةَ لَمْ أَخِمْ
لَوْ كَانَ يَدْرِي مَا المُحَاوَرَةُ اشْتَكَى

translationEn: I feared I would die before war had turned its wheel upon the two sons of Damdam — those who reviled my honor though I had not reviled them, and who vowed my blood if they did not meet me. If they carry out their threat, well, I had already left their father as carrion for the wild beasts and every bald-headed vulture. When they shielded themselves behind me against the spearpoints, I did not flinch, though the path ahead narrowed. My horse twisted aside from the spear-strikes at his chest and complained to me with a tear and a whinny. Had he known what conversation was, he would have protested; had he known speech, he would have spoken to me.
context: From the battle section of Antara's Mu'allaqa, featuring the famous passage where Antara addresses his horse, who weeps and whinnies under the rain of spears. This humanization of the warhorse became a hallmark of Antara's poetry.

titleEn: Proclaim My Glory

verses.sadr: أَنَا فِي الحَرْبِ العَوَانِ غَيْرُ مُحِلِّ
إِنِّي امْرُؤٌ مِنْ خَيْرِ عَبْسٍ مَنْصِبًا

translationEn: Do not give me the water of life in humiliation — rather, give me with honor the cup of bitter colocynth. The water of life in humiliation is like hellfire, and hellfire with honor is the finest abode. I am one who in fierce battle never permits flight, and in close combat we have a mighty hand. I am a man of the noblest lineage in Abs on one side, and I protect the other side with my unsheathed blade.
context: Among Antara's most famous stand-alone verses expressing his philosophy of honor above life itself. The couplet about preferring the bitter cup of honor to the sweet water of humiliation is one of the most quoted lines in all of Arabic poetry, often cited as a proverb.

titleEn: Longing for Abla
translationEn: O dwelling of Abla at al-Jiwa, speak to me! Good morning, dwelling of Abla, and be safe! I fell in love with her by chance while I was slaying her people — by your father's life, that is no empty claim. You have settled in my heart in the station of a beloved held in highest honor. How can I visit when her people have camped at Unayza-tayn while ours are at al-Ghaylam? If you have resolved upon departure, your riding camels were bridled in the darkness of night.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I became attached to her (in love)
visiting, the possibility of a visit

context: Antara's verses about his impossible love for Abla, his paternal cousin. His mixed-race status (his mother was an Abyssinian slave) meant he was denied the right to marry her under tribal custom. The paradox of killing her tribesmen while loving her became a central theme of his poetry.

titleAr: كان مغرما بابنة عمه عبلة فقل أن تخلو له قصيدة من ذكرها. اجتمع في شبابه بامرئ القيس الشاعر،
titleEn: He Was Enamored with His Cousin Abla

verses.sadr: كان مغرما بابنة عمه عبلة فقل أن تخلو له قصيدة من ذكرها. اجتمع في شبابه بامرئ القيس الشاعر،
verses.ajuz: وشهد حرب داحس والغبراء، وعاش طويلا، وقتله الأسد الرهيص أو جبار بن عمرو الطائيديوان عنترة بن شداد

verses.sadr: أشهر فرسان العرب في الجاهلية ومن شعراء الطبقة الأولى. من أهل نجد. أمه حبشية اسمها زبيبة، سرى إليه
verses.ajuz: السواد منها. وكان من أحسن العرب شيمة ومن أعزهم نفسا، يوصف بالحلم على شدة بطشه، وفي شعره رقة وعذوبة.

verses.sadr: كان مغرما بابنة عمه عبلة فقل أن تخلو له قصيدة من ذكرها. اجتمع في شبابه بامرئ القيس
verses.ajuz: الشاعر، وشهد حرب داحس والغبراء، وعاش طويلا، وقتله الأسد الرهيص أو جبار بن عمرو الطائي

translationEn: He was passionately in love with his cousin Abla, and scarcely a poem of his was free from mention of her. In his youth he met the poet Imru al-Qays, witnessed the war of Dahis and al-Ghabra, and lived a long life. He was slain by al-Asad al-Rahis or by Jabbar ibn Amr al-Ta'i. Antara ibn Shaddad ibn Amr ibn Muawiya ibn Qurrad al-Absi: the most famous knight of the Arabs in the age of ignorance and among poets of the first rank. From the people of Najd, his mother was an Abyssinian woman named Zubaiba, from whom he inherited his dark complexion. He was among the finest of Arabs in character and the most self-respecting, described as forbearing despite his fierce strength, and his poetry combined tenderness and sweetness.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the war of Dahis and al-Ghabra (famous pre-Islamic conflict)
the pre-Islamic era, Age of Ignorance

context: This is the introductory biographical notice preceding Antara's diwan, describing his lineage, character, loves, and death. It serves as the classical preface to his collected poems.

titleAr: إن كان يسعدني الزمان فإنني في همتي لصروفه
titleEn: If Fate Should Aid Me in My Resolve

verses.sadr: إن كان يسعدني الزمان فإنني في همتي لصروفه
verses.ajuz: أرزاءرمت الفؤاد مليحة عذراء بسهام لحظ ما لهن دواء

verses.sadr: يا عبل مثل هواك أو أضعافه

translationEn: If fate should aid me, then in my resolve I am a calamity to its vicissitudes. A beautiful virgin struck my heart with the arrows of her glances — arrows for which there is no cure. She passed during the festival season among full-bosomed maidens like suns, their glances those of gazelles. My hidden sickness overtook me — what I had concealed, concealment itself revealed. She swayed and I said: a willow branch stirred by the south wind in the morning breeze. She gazed and I said: a startled gazelle, frightened in the wasteland by some calamity. She appeared and I said: the full moon adorned by the constellation Orion. She smiled and the pearl-light of her teeth shone — in it is a cure for the disease of lovers. She bowed in worship of her Lord and the great lords bowed in awe of her majesty. O Abla, love for you — or twice as much — remains in me as hope even when despair has fallen.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a willow branch (simile for graceful gait)

context: A ghazal combining Antara's characteristic love for Abla with elaborate similes — comparing her to the moon, a willow, and a gazelle — alongside his defiant spirit against the cruelties of time.

titleAr: فلئن بقيت لأصنعن عجائبا ولأبكمنن بلاغة الفصحاءما
titleEn: If I Live I Shall Perform Wonders

verses.sadr: فلئن بقيت لأصنعن عجائبا ولأبكمنن بلاغة الفصحاءما
verses.ajuz: دمت مرتقيا إلى العلياء حتى بلغت إلى ذرى الجوزاء

verses.sadr: فهناك لا ألوي على من لامني

verses.ajuz: أرى ما أرتجيه أو يحين قضائي

verses.sadr: من كان يجحدني فقد برح الخفا

translationEn: If I live, I shall perform wonders and silence the eloquence of the eloquent — so long as I climb toward the heights until I reach the peaks of Orion. There I shall not turn back from those who blame me, in fear of death or separation from the living. I shall enrage my critics and my enviers, and I shall endure rejection and abandonment with patience. I shall strive toward the encounter, that I might see what I hope for, or that my end might come. I shall guard my soul from its desires until I am seen as one of commitment and loyalty. Whoever denies me — the hidden has now emerged, what I used to conceal from the watchful. My dark color and the name of Zubaiba have not troubled me, when my enemies have fallen short of my resolve.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I shall surely silence, make speechless
my critics, those who rebuke me
the watchers, the watchful (pl. of رقيب)

context: A bold fakhr poem where Antara vows to silence his detractors and achieve greatness despite his mixed-race origins. His defiance against mockery of his dark skin and Abyssinian mother is a recurring theme.

titleAr: ولكن تبعد الفحشاء عني كبعد الأرض عن جو السماءلئن
titleEn: But Vulgarity Stays Far from Me

verses.sadr: ولكن تبعد الفحشاء عني كبعد الأرض عن جو السماءلئن
verses.ajuz: أك أسودا فالمسك لوني وما لسواد جلدي من دواء

translationEn: But vulgarity is kept far from me, as far as the earth is from the vault of heaven. Even if I am dark-skinned, musk is my color — and for the darkness of my skin there is no cure (nor do I seek one).

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the vault of heaven, the sky's expanse
musk (the finest and most prized fragrance)

context: A short but striking fragment where Antara turns the mockery of his dark skin into a badge of honor by comparing his color to musk, the most prized of fragrances.

titleAr: يا طامعا في هلاكي عد بلا طمع ولا ترد كأس حتف أنت
titleEn: O You Who Crave My Ruin, Turn Back

verses.sadr: يا طامعا في هلاكي عد بلا طمع ولا ترد كأس حتف أنت
verses.ajuz: شاربهكم يبعد الدهر من أرجو أقاربه عني ويبعث شيطانا أحاربه
طبائعه فكيف يهنا به حر يصاحبه

verses.sadr: كم ليلة سرت في البيداء منفردا
وكم غدير مزجت الماء فيه دما
يا طامعا في هلاكي عد بلا
verses.ajuz: طمع ولا ترد كأس حتف أنت شاربه

translationEn: O you who crave my destruction, turn back without your craving — do not approach the cup of doom, for you are the one who will drink it. How time distances from me those I hope as kin, and sends a devil for me to fight. What an age — whenever its turns end, its consequences ravage us. An age that sees treachery as part of its nature — how can a free man find comfort in its company? I experienced it naively, and its trials refined me after they had whitened my hair. How should I fear time's calamities when calamity is the least of what I hold against time? How many nights did I travel alone in the wilderness while the stars tilted toward the west. My sword was my companion and my spear — whenever the lion of the lair roared, it turned aside from me. How many a pool I mixed with blood at dawn while wild beasts came seeking it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one who covets, one who craves greedily
cup of doom, draught of death
reversals of fortune, vicissitudes of time
the wilderness, the vast empty desert

context: Antara confronts an enemy who seeks his downfall with scornful defiance, then reflects on the treacherous nature of time and his solitary life as a warrior who sleeps with his sword as his only companion.

titleAr: والنقع يوم طراد الخيل يشهد لي والضرب والطعن والأقلام والكتبلا
titleEn: The Battle Dust Bears Witness for Me

verses.sadr: والنقع يوم طراد الخيل يشهد لي والضرب والطعن والأقلام والكتبلا
verses.ajuz: يحمل الحقد من تعلو به الرتب ولا ينال العلى من طبعه الغضب

verses.sadr: ومن يكن عبد قوم لا يخالفهم
قد كنت فيما مضى أرعى جمالهم
لله در بني عبس لقد نسلوا
verses.ajuz: من الأكارم ما قد تنسل العرب

verses.sadr: لئن يعيبوا سوادي فهو لي نسب
verses.ajuz: يوم النزال إذا ما فاتني النسب

verses.sadr: إن كنت تعلم يا نعمان أي فتى
verses.ajuz: يلقى أخاك الذي قد غره العصب

verses.sadr: لا أبعد الله عن عيني غطارفة
verses.ajuz: إنسا إذا نزلوا جنا إذا ركبوا

verses.sadr: أسود غاب ولكن لا نيوب لهم
ما زلت ألقى صدور الخيل مندفقا
فا لعمي لو كان في أجفانهم نظروا
verses.ajuz: والخرس لو كان في أفواههم خطبوا

verses.sadr: والنقع يوم طراد الخيل يشهد لي

translationEn: The battle dust on the day of cavalry pursuit bears witness for me, as do the strikes and the thrusts and the pens and the books. He who has risen in rank does not carry grudges, and he whose nature is anger does not attain glory. He who is the slave of a people does not oppose them when they mistreat him, but seeks their pleasure when they rebuke him. I used to herd their camels in the past — today I protect their territory whenever they suffer hardship. By God's blessing, the sons of Abs — they have produced among the noble what the Arabs have produced. Even if they fault my dark complexion, it is my lineage on the day of combat when lineage fails me. Know, O Nu'man, what youth it is who will meet your brother whom clan pride has deceived — a youth who wades into battle's depths smiling and returns while the spear-tip is drenched in blood. When his sharp sword is drawn its edges blaze and the horizon brightens and veils are rent apart. The horses bear witness that I hold them back while the thrusting blazes like fire's sparks. When I meet enemies on the day of battle I leave their deluded host being plundered. The souls are mine, the flesh for the birds, the bones for the wild beasts, and the spoils for the horsemen.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: battle dust, the dust raised by armies
noble warriors, generous chiefs (pl. of غطريف)
noble mares (descended from the horse Aawaj)
wolves (pl. of سرحان), here simile for speed
Indian swords, slender blades (pl. of قضيب)
the breast-strap of a horse's saddle

context: One of Antara's major fakhr poems addressed to al-Nu'man, invoking the testimony of battle dust, swords, pens, and books to his valor. He transforms his servile past into a proof of his exceptional loyalty and strength.

titleAr: قتلنا منهم مائتين حرا وألفا في الشعاب وفي الهضابألا
titleEn: We Slew Two Hundred of Them Free Men

verses.sadr: قتلنا منهم مائتين حرا وألفا في الشعاب وفي الهضابألا
verses.ajuz: ياعبل قد زاد التصابي ولج اليوم قومك في عذابي

verses.sadr: سلي يا عبل عنا يوم زرنا

translationEn: We slew two hundred free men of them and a thousand in the ravines and on the hills. O Abla! My youthful longing has intensified, and today your people persist in tormenting me. Your love continues to grow each day, just as the grey in my hair grows amid my youth. I have reproved the reverses of time regarding you until — by your father — my whole life has been spent in reproach. I met the enemies and protected a people who abandoned me and did not guard my honor. Ask about us, O Abla, on the day we visited the tribes of Amir and Banu Kilab. How many a horseman did I leave fallen, his palms reddened without henna. He moves his leg in terror while the spear-point gleams within him like a meteor.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: youthful longing, acting as if young in love
the reverses of my time, fortune's vicissitudes
my honor, my side, my protection
with dyed/reddened palms (from blood, not henna)
a meteor, a shooting star, a firebrand

context: Antara combines the two great themes of his poetry — war glory and love for Abla — lamenting that despite his incredible military victories, his people neglect him and Abla remains beyond reach.

titleAr: ولا تسقني كأس المدام فإنها يضل بها عقل الشجاع ويذهبسلا
titleEn: Do Not Pour Me Wine for It Leads Brave Men Astray

verses.sadr: ولا تسقني كأس المدام فإنها يضل بها عقل الشجاع ويذهبسلا
verses.ajuz: القلب عما كان يهوى ويطلب وأصبح لا يشكو ولا يتعتب

verses.sadr: صحا بعد سكر وانتخى بعد ذلة
إلى كم أداري من تريد مذلتي
فلا تحسبي أني على البعد نادم
verses.ajuz: ولا القلب في نار الغرام معذب

verses.sadr: وقد قلت إني قد سلوت عن الهوى
verses.ajuz: ومن كان مثلي لا يقول ويكذب

verses.sadr: لقد ذل من أمسى على ربع منزل
وقد فاز من في الحرب أصبح
نديمي رعاك الله قم غن لي على
verses.ajuz: كؤوس المنايا من دم حين أشرب

translationEn: Do not pour me wine, for it leads astray the brave man's mind and destroys it. My heart has consoled itself from what it used to desire and seek, and now neither complains nor protests. It sobered after intoxication and rose in pride after abasement — the heart of one who desires glory is ever turning. How long shall I coddle one who wants my humiliation, spending all my effort in her satisfaction while she remains angry? O Ubayyla! The days of beauty are few — they have a known reign and then pass away. Do not think I regret the distance, nor that my heart is tormented in passion's fire. I have said I have consoled myself from love — but one like me does not say it and then lie. I have abandoned you, so go wherever you wish and try someone other than me — the wise man tries. Truly humbled is he who at nightfall weeps over the ruins of a dwelling, lamenting traces of homes. But triumphant is he who in battle roams, thrusting at his rival while the dust-clouds rise high.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: rose in pride, became proud and dignified
I coddle, I handle gently, I humor
I consoled myself, I forgot, I moved on
cups of death (the blood of battle instead of wine)

context: Antara refuses wine and instead drinks from the cups of battle. He also adopts a defiant posture toward Abla, claiming he has consoled himself — though he admits the claim itself is a lie, a moment of rare emotional honesty.

titleAr: وغادرت نضلة في معرك يجر الأسنة
titleEn: I Left Nudla in a Battle Dragging Spears

verses.sadr: وغادرت نضلة في معرك يجر الأسنة
verses.ajuz: كالمحتطبيذبب ورد على إثره وأمكنه وقع مرد خشب

translationEn: I left Nudla on the battlefield, dragging the spears like a woodcutter. A blond horse followed in his tracks and struck him with the blow of a thick wooden mace. Pursuit unceasing, seeking nothing else, with a white sword like a blazing firebrand. Let whoever doubts his killing know that Abu Nawfal has perished. I left Nudla on the battlefield dragging the spears like a woodcutter.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I left behind, I abandoned (in death)
the woodcutter (simile: dragging spears like logs)
a firebrand, a burning brand taken from fire
perished, was destroyed, came to ruin

context: A brief battle fragment commemorating the killing of an enemy named Nudla (Abu Nawfal), described with Antara's characteristic vivid imagery of pursuit and slaughter.

titleAr: كتائب تزجى فوق كل كتيبة لواء كظل الطائر المتقلبكأن
titleEn: Battalions Driving Above Each Battalion a Banner

verses.sadr: كتائب تزجى فوق كل كتيبة لواء كظل الطائر المتقلبكأن
verses.ajuz: السرايا بين قو وقارة عصائب طير ينتحين لمشرب

verses.sadr: وقد كنت أخشى أن أموت ولم
verses.ajuz: تقم قرائب عمرو وسط نوح مسلب

translationEn: Battalions are driven forward, above each battalion a banner like the shadow of a soaring bird. The raiding parties between Qaw and Qara were like flocks of birds heading to a watering place. I used to fear I might die before Amr's kinswomen stood amid wailing, their garments stripped in mourning. What healed my soul — or came close to healing it — was toppling them from a high precipice. The Rudaynian spears cry out in their coats of mail like the cry of lance-shafts in the pierced straightening frames.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: are driven forward, are urged along
Rudaynian spears (attributed to Rudayna, a renowned spear-maker)
a frame used to straighten warped spear-shafts

context: A fragment of a battle poem where Antara describes the splendor and terror of marching armies, comparing battalions to flocking birds and confessing a private fear of dying before achieving revenge.

titleAr: وأنا امرؤ إن يأخذوني عنوة أقرن إلى شرالركاب وأجنبلا
titleEn: I Am a Man Who If Taken by Force

verses.sadr: وأنا امرؤ إن يأخذوني عنوة أقرن إلى شرالركاب وأجنبلا
verses.ajuz: تذكري مهري وما أطعمته فيكون جلدك مثل جلد الأجرب

translationEn: I am a man — if they take me by force, I will be bound to the worst of mounts and led aside. Do not mention my horse and what I fed it, lest your skin become like the skin of a mangy camel. The evening drink is for him, while you suffer — so sigh all you wish and moan your grief. Old wine and cold water from a skin — if you are asking me for an evening drink, then leave. Men have a way to you — if they take you, then put on your kohl and henna. Your mount will be a hobbled camel with its pack, while that day my mount will be Ibn al-Na'ama (a swift horse). I fear my wife will say: here rises a soaring dust-cloud — so gird yourself for battle! And I am a man — if they take me by force, I will be bound to the worst of mounts and led aside.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: by force, compulsorily, against one's will
the mangy camel, a camel with mange disease
the evening drink (of milk or wine)
my wife in her howdah, a woman on a camel

context: A poem where Antara debates with his wife (or companion) about the horse's right to feed before human comfort, revealing his warrior priorities, and ends with his defiant pride — preferring death to captivity.

titleAr: ودعوني أجر ذيل فخار عندما تخجل الجبان
titleEn: Let Me Trail the Robe of Glory

verses.sadr: ودعوني أجر ذيل فخار عندما تخجل الجبان
verses.ajuz: العيوبحسناتي عند الزمان ذنوب وفعالي مذمة وعيوب

verses.sadr: إن طيف الخيال يا عبل يشفي
كل يوم له عتاب مع الده
كم شجاع دنا إلي ونادى يا

verses.ajuz: الأر ض وقد شقت عليه الجيوب

verses.sadr: وهو يحمي معي على كل قرن

translationEn: Leave me to trail the robe of glory when the coward's defects make him blush. My virtues in the eyes of time are sins, and my deeds are deemed blame and faults. My share of the beloved is distance, while for others proximity is the lot. Each day sickness withers a lover away from the beloved — and for my sickness there is no physician. Time seems to love a beloved, and I seem to watch over time as a guardian. O Abla, the phantom of the imagination heals and treats my sorrowful heart. My death in love is lighter for me than living when the beloved has abandoned me. O breeze of the Hijaz, were it not for you extinguishing the fire of my heart, longing would melt my body. From me when you breathe come warmth, and from your fragrance comes Ubayyla's perfume. A dove cooed in the branches and stirred my yearning with its lament. It spent the night lamenting a distant mate, calling out: I am the lonely stranger. O branch-dwelling dove, if you were a lover like me, no tender branch would comfort you. Leave grief and passion for a lover whose heart torment has melted. Each day he quarrels with fate and faces matters that perplex the wise. Endless calamities and sorrows with no conclusion, and troubles without end. Ask about me, O Ubayyla, from one who knows — a warrior whom wars have turned grey. He will tell you that in the edge of my sword death's angel stands ever-present. And my spear knows the armored men well — ask it what hearts are made of. How many a brave man came close to me, crying: O my people, I am the fearsome warrior! He had no sooner called than he went biting the earth with his garments torn. To me the brown spears are kin, and my horse — when I call it, it answers. The sword laughs in my hand and calls, while in another's grasp it weeps. It protects me against every rival just as kinsman protects kinsman. So leave me from drinking a cup of wine among companions of grace and perfume. And leave me to trail the robe of glory when the coward's defects make him blush.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to trail the robe of glory (idiom for walking in pride)
the phantom, the specter of imagination (poetic visitation of the beloved in dreams)
the breeze of the Hijaz (bringing news of the beloved)
armored warriors, those wearing coats of mail
biting the earth (idiom for falling dead in battle)

context: One of Antara's longer poems combining ghazal pathos — addressed to Abla and the Hijaz breeze — with fierce fakhr boasting. The sword that laughs in his hand but weeps in another's is among his most memorable images.

titleAr: فمن أجاب نجا مما يحاذره ومن أبى طعم الحرب والحربدعني
titleEn: Whoever Answers Is Saved from What He Fears

verses.sadr: فمن أجاب نجا مما يحاذره ومن أبى طعم الحرب والحربدعني
verses.ajuz: أجد إلى العلياء في الطلب وأبلغ الغاية القصوى من الرتب
تزور شعري بركن البيت في رجب

verses.sadr: يا عبل قومي انظري فعلي ولا تسلي

verses.ajuz: عين الوليد إليه شاب وهو صبي
بصارمي لا بأمي لا ولا بأبي

translationEn: Whoever responds is saved from what he fears, and whoever refuses gets the taste of war — and war it is. Leave me to strive eagerly toward glory and reach the furthest heights of rank. Perhaps Abla will awaken pleased with my dark complexion and the image of anger will fade from her face — when she sees all the lords making pilgrimage to honor my poetry at the corner of the House in Rajab. O Abla, rise and witness my deeds, and do not ask about me the envious one who would tell you lies. If the eyes of warriors fix their gaze upon me and every bold fighter of war turns to flee — I leave them no face for the routed man and no path to save them from destruction. Hasten and witness a thrust — when the child al-Walid looked upon it, he aged though he was a boy. I was created for war: I protect it when it cools and I warm myself at its fire when its blaze is fierce. With a sharp sword — wherever I draw it, the tyrants of Persia and the Arabs prostrate. I have sought a high station by my sword — not by my mother, not by my father.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: what he fears, what he is wary of
the ultimate goal, the furthest limit
tyrants, mighty ones, great rulers (pl. of جبار)
prostrated (metaphor: enemies fell or bowed in submission)

context: Antara declares his path to glory is his sword alone — not birth, not parentage. He challenges Abla to witness his deeds directly rather than listen to envious tongues, a recurring motif in his self-assertive poetry.

titleAr: مقامك في جو السماء مكانه وباعي قصير عن نوال الكواكبأعاتب
titleEn: Your Station Is in the Vault of Heaven

verses.sadr: مقامك في جو السماء مكانه وباعي قصير عن نوال الكواكبأعاتب
verses.ajuz: دهرا لا يلين لعاتب وأطلب أمنا من صروف النوائب

verses.sadr: ينادونني في السلم يا بن زبيبة
verses.ajuz: وعند صدام الخيل يا ابن الأطايب

verses.sadr: ولولا الهوى ما ذل مثلي لمثلهم
فيا ليت أن الدهر يدني أحبتي
وليت خيالا منك يا عبل طارقا

translationEn: Your station is set in the vault of heaven and my arm is too short to reach the stars. I reproach a time that never softens to the one who reproaches, and I seek safety from the reversals of calamity. The days promise me promises that deceive me, and I know for certain that they are false promises. I served people and took them as kin, hoping they would help — but they turned out like scorpions. In peace they call me: O son of Zubaiba! But at the clash of horses: O son of the noble ones! Were it not for love, one like me would never humiliate himself before their like — the lion of the wilderness would not bow to foxes. My people will remember me when the horses begin to roam between the camp-tents with riders. If they forget me, the swords and the spears will remind them of my deeds and the strikes of my blades. Would that time brought my loved ones near me as it brings near to me my calamities! Would that a phantom of you, O Abla, visiting by night, could see the overflow of my eyelids with falling tears. I shall be patient until my critics abandon me and until patience itself cries out between my ribs.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: my arm is short (idiom: beyond my reach)
reaching the stars, attaining what is unreachable
my critics, those who rebuke and blame me

context: Antara laments that Abla is as unreachable as the stars, while simultaneously exposing the hypocrisy of his tribe who call him 'son of a slave woman' in peace but 'son of noble men' in battle when they need him.

titleAr: وأترك كل نائحة تنادي عليهم بالتفرق والشتاتإذا
titleEn: I Leave Every Wailing Woman Calling upon Them

verses.sadr: وأترك كل نائحة تنادي عليهم بالتفرق والشتاتإذا
verses.ajuz: قنع الفتى بذميم عيش وكان وراء سجف كالبنات

translationEn: I leave every wailing woman crying out for them, calling dispersion and scattering upon them — when a youth settles for a shameful life and sits behind a curtain like the girls. When he does not storm the lions of death and does not thrust at the chests of the noble horses. When he does not honor guests when they come to him and does not quench the swords from warriors. When he achieves no glory by striking heads and is not patient in calamities. Then say to the wailing women when they weep for him: shorter your laments, O mourning women! Do not mourn anyone but a lion of the thicket, brave in the raging wars. Let me in battle die with honor — for a death in glory is better than my life. By my life, pride is not in acquiring wealth, nor is the generous chief called the merely rich. The battles will remember me at every moment through all of life until death. That remembrance remains and does not perish through all the days past and to come. Today I protect my people's honor and support the house of Abs against the enemies. And I take back what is ours by a war that makes the backs of firm mountains fall.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a wailing woman, a female mourner
noble horses standing with one hoof raised (pl. of صافن)
armored warriors, helmeted fighters (pl. of كمي)
mourning women who lament aloud (pl. of نادبة)
battles, great clashes, tumultuous fights (pl. of معمعة)
firm mountains, steadfast heights (pl. of راسية)

context: Antara articulates his philosophy of honor: he reserves mourning only for true warriors and requests a death in glory rather than a shameful life of ease. Fame in battle is the only true wealth for a man of the swords.

titleAr: ولي بيت علا فلك الثريا تخر لعظم هيبته
titleEn: I Have a House That Towers above the Pleiades

verses.sadr: ولي بيت علا فلك الثريا تخر لعظم هيبته
verses.ajuz: البيوتسكت فغر أعدائي السكوت وظنوني لأهلي قد نسيت

translationEn: I have a house that has risen beyond the sphere of the Pleiades — the houses fall prostrate before the greatness of its awe. I fell silent and my enemies opened wide their mouths in silence, thinking I had forgotten my people. How can I sleep regarding the lords of a people in whose bounty's grace I was raised? Whenever their enemies' horses encircled them and they called out to me, I answered whenever I was called — with a sword whose edge is the wave of death, and a spear whose chest is the fatal end. I was created from iron, stronger of heart — for iron has worn out but I have not worn out. I have drunk the blood of enemies from the skulls of their heads and I have not been quenched. I was born an infant in the midst of renewed war and suckled on the milk of great battles. The spear has no share in my body and the sword finds no sustenance in my limbs. And I have a house that has risen beyond the sphere of the Pleiades — the houses fall prostrate before the greatness of its awe.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: awe, grandeur, the quality of inspiring reverence
doom, death (from natural or violent causes)
wore out, deteriorated with age and use
the skulls of heads (used as drinking bowls, a hyperbolic image)
renewed war, a second or long-running conflict

context: Antara boasts of his transcendent glory — born in battle, suckled on war, impervious to iron — and of his unshakeable loyalty to Banu Abs despite their mistreatment of him.

titleAr: ألا إنها خير القصائد كلها يفصل منها كل ثوب
titleEn: Truly This Is the Best of All Poems

verses.sadr: ألا إنها خير القصائد كلها يفصل منها كل ثوب
verses.ajuz: وينسجأشاقك من عبل الخيال المبهج فقلبك فيه لاعج يتوهج
ديار التي في حبها بت ألهج

verses.sadr: ألا هل ترى إن شط عني مزارها
وقد سرت يا بنت الكرام مبادرا

verses.ajuz: كأن لم يكن فيها من العيش مبهج
إلى أن بدا ضوء الصباح المبلج
على غارة من مثلها الخيل تسرج
ترى حببا من فوقها حين تمزج

verses.sadr: وإني لأحمي الجار من كل ذلة
وأحمي حمى قومي على طول مدتي
verses.ajuz: الى أن يروني في اللفائف أدرج
يلوح لها ضوء من الصبح أبلج

translationEn: Truly this is the best of all poems — every garment of rhetoric is cut and woven from it. Did Abla's dazzling phantom stir your longing? Your heart within it burns and blazes. I have lost her who departed, and I spent the night tormented, for a howdah carried her away from you toward separation. It was as if my heart on the day I stood bidding Ubayyla farewell was a fugitive wriggling free from me. O my two companions, I have not forgotten you — may my father and her father ransom you — where, where is the path of ascent? Pass by the water of the Dahradin and recall the abodes of her in whose love I spent my nights in earnest. The dwellings where Abla of the curtained chambers sheltered — now the four wild winds howl through them. Will you see how her dwelling place has grown distant, and one who disturbs has dislodged her from her people? Can a young she-camel, swift and easy in her gait through the wastes, bring me to her abode? She shows you when she turns her back a hump and withers, and when she faces you a chest that ripples. O Ubayyla, this is a pearl-necklace of verse I have strung — and you are its string and its beauty and its path. I rode out O daughter of the noble ones with haste, beneath me my camel-horse reckless among camels. Through a land where water pours on its plateaus so its plants blazed forth, where myrtle and lote-tree and ghada-wood and buckthorn and sweet-briar and rose and Christ's-thorn leafed green. Though the ruins of that land have become empty as if no joyful life ever dwelt in them — yet how long did I joke with Ubayyla there, and the coquettish gazelle joked with me: a nasal-voiced creature of graceful allure, large-eyed and kohl-rimmed, arched of brow, pure-cheeked, of bright forehead and deep black eyes. With an eyebrow like the nun-letter above eyelids, and a gap-toothed smile like white chamomile flowers. With heavy hips and a slender swaying waist, a rose-cheeked cheek and a plump firm leg. With a belly like the fold of a Saberian garment, tender and flat, slim-flanked and trim. I sported with her while the night let down its curtains, until the light of clear morning appeared. I watched the night's stars as if they were flasks with mercury quivering inside. Under me her forearm with its gleaming bracelet, above me another forearm with its bracelet. True brothers, sincere companions whom I accompanied on a raid for which horses are saddled. A dark aged wine circulated among them, its bubbles visible when it was mixed. Truly it is excellent medicine for one who drinks — pour it for me before you leave. And we spent our morning drunk, the wine arrayed, poured around us, and the elaborate food. What alarmed me on the day of combat was a man rushing at me drenched as if stained in saffron. He rushed at me with his throat ringing, sometimes closing in, sometimes trotting. When he drew near I cut his aorta with the edge of a sharp gleaming sword. The horse's blood when it poured was like the perfume of virgins or an embroidered tent. Woe to Khosrow if I march through his land, and woe to the Persian army when I roar! I charge them with an Antaran charge that drives heroes into the wilderness howling. I ram the people's champion and make him taste the bitterness of death's cup biting without spitting. I take vengeance for the noble lord of his people and kindle in the battle a blazing fire. Truly I bear every calamity at which lofty mountains fall and are dismayed. Truly I protect my neighbor from every humiliation and rejoice and delight in the visiting guest. I protect my people's sanctuary through all my days until they see me wrapped in shrouds. Here for you, O House of Abs, is an ode whose light shines like the brightness of clear dawn. Truly this is the best of all poems — every garment of rhetoric is cut and woven from it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a howdah, a covered litter on a camel for women
a young swift she-camel, nimble and fast
the aorta, the main artery of the heart
a saffron-based perfume used by women
a great calamity, a heavy burden

context: One of Antara's longest and most celebrated qasidas, opening with a declaration of its own supremacy. It moves through the traditional sequence: nasib (longing for Abla), rahil (desert journey), and fakhr (battle boasting), culminating in threats against Khosrow of Persia.

titleAr: كسوت الجعد جعد بني أبان سلاحي بعد عري
titleEn: I Clothed the Curly-Haired One of Banu Aban with My Armor

verses.sadr: كسوت الجعد جعد بني أبان سلاحي بعد عري
verses.ajuz: وافتضاحإذا لاقيت جمع بني أبان فإني لائم للجعد لاح

translationEn: I clothed the curly-haired one — the curly-haired of Banu Aban — with my armor after his nakedness and disgrace. When I meet the gathering of Banu Aban, I am one who blames the curly-haired openly. It is as if the ornament on the upper arms is a partridge strutting between beautiful pools. He was entrusted with my favor and rewarded it by going out early or hastening in the evening. Do you not know — may God requite you — that I am bold when I meet men of the spears? I clothed the curly-haired one — the curly-haired of Banu Aban — with my armor after his nakedness and disgrace.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the curly-haired one (a nickname or descriptor)
disgrace, scandal, public exposure of shame
a partridge (simile for graceful, preening movement)
strutting, walking with a swaying gait

context: A poem of reproach addressed to a man of Banu Aban whom Antara helped by equipping him with armor, only to be betrayed. It reflects Antara's code of honor and his sharp memory for ingratitude.

titleAr: يجررن هاما فلقتها رماحنا تزيل منهن اللحى والمسايحطربت
titleEn: They Drag Skulls Our Spears Have Split

verses.sadr: يجررن هاما فلقتها رماحنا تزيل منهن اللحى والمسايحطربت
verses.ajuz: وهاجتك الظباء السوانح غداة غدت منها سنيح وبارح
بزندين في جوفي من الوجد قادح

verses.sadr: وقد كنت تخفي حب سمراء حقبة
verses.ajuz: فبح لان منها بالذي أنت بائح

verses.sadr: أعاذل كم من يوم حرب شهدته
فلم أر حيا صابروا مثل صبرنا
وسارت رجال نحو أخرى عليهم الح
إذا ما مشوا في السابغات حسبتهم
ودرنا كما دارت على قطبها الرحى

verses.ajuz: لها منبت في آل ضبة طامح

translationEn: They drag skulls our spears have split, removing from them the beards and the temple-locks. You were stirred and the gazelles — those passing to the right and those passing to the left — excited you at dawn when they went forth. Longing rose so high in me that it was as if two fire-sticks were striking flame in my chest from yearning. You used to hide your love for the dark-complexioned one for a time — now reveal what you are revealing. By my life, I would have had excuse were you to excuse me — you roughened a heart whose innermost was once a counselor to you. O you who rebuke me, how many a day of battle have I witnessed with a sight of bared teeth grimacing. I have seen no people who endured as we endured, nor faced battle as we face those we battle. Whenever he wished, an armored warrior met me on a swift horse, giving free rein to thrusting. We advanced in a slow march or met a battalion that traded thrusts with us, or a crier frightened the cattle. When we met at al-Jifar they were shattered, and the garrison fell back on its own tracks. Men marched toward others — on them the iron lay heavy as laden camels walk. When they walked in full coats of mail I thought them torrents that had flooded the plains. Banners were raised and under their shade stood the people who are sons of the great wars. We wheeled as the millstone wheels on its pivot, and swords wheeled on the heads of men. Through the midday heat until its light vanished, and a night came on that clenched the distant eye. Banu Abs called to one another with every Indian sword, a blade that removes the skull while the rank tilts. And every Rudaynian spear whose tip was like a meteor appearing bright in the darkness of night. They abandoned to us the protected women and fled scattered — some straight and some bolting. And every full-ankled woman of fleshy shins, of lofty lineage among the house of Dabba. We left Dirar between a shackled captive and a slain man whose wailing women were absent. And Amr and Hayyan we left in a wilderness where grinning hyenas grew accustomed to them.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: temple-locks, locks of hair at the sides of the face (pl. of مسيحة)
omens passing from left to right (considered favorable in pre-Islamic belief)
fully armed, clad in complete armor
full coats of chain mail (pl. of سابغة)
grinning, showing teeth (said of hyenas and the desert wind)
the protected women, women under the guard of their tribe

context: A long battle poem opening with a striking image of horses dragging split skulls, combined with a love prelude and a detailed account of a battle against Banu Dabba and other tribes, ending with enemies left to the hyenas.

titleAr: فقد أمكنت منك الأسنة عانيا فلم تجز إذ تسعى قتيلا
titleEn: The Spears Have Got You Captive

verses.sadr: فقد أمكنت منك الأسنة عانيا فلم تجز إذ تسعى قتيلا
verses.ajuz: بمعبدنحا فارس الشهباء والخيل جنح على فارس بين الأسنة مقصد
ولا تأمنن مايحدث الله في غد

verses.sadr: فإن يك عبد الله لاقى فوارسا

verses.ajuz: فلم تجز إذ تسعى قتيلا بمعبد

translationEn: The spears have gotten you as a captive — you could not cross when you rushed forward slain before Mu'abad. The rider of the grey horse veered, and the horses tilted over a rider amid the spears, targeted. Were it not for a hand of ours that reached him, the wild beasts would have passed his dismembered limbs among themselves without him being propped. So do not be ungrateful for the favor — extol its grace, and do not feel secure from what God will bring forth tomorrow. For if Abdullah met horsemen who repel the flash of the blazing clouds — the spears have gotten you as a captive: you could not cross when you rushed forward slain before Mu'abad.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a captive, a prisoner (adj. from عانٍ)
you could cross, you could pass (تجوز)

context: A short poem in which Antara reminds someone (likely a former enemy or one he saved) of the favor he did by sparing his life. He warns against ingratitude and complacency about the future.

titleAr: قصائد من قيل امرىء يحتذيكم بني العشراء فارتدوا
titleEn: Poems of a Man Whose Example You Should Follow

verses.sadr: قصائد من قيل امرىء يحتذيكم بني العشراء فارتدوا
verses.ajuz: وتقلدواهديكم خير أبا من أبيكم أعف وأوفى بالجوار وأحمد

translationEn: Poems from the saying of a man you should emulate — O sons of al-Ashara, cloak yourselves and gird yourselves with them. Your guide is a man better than your father: more chaste, more faithful in neighborly protection, and more praiseworthy. Who thrusts in the fray when the horses turn back in the morning from the targeted Samhari spear. Why did not Amr ibn Jabir in the clamor stand by his oath, and the son of the foundling woman prove a stubborn one? From me — though I be far — will come to you the smoke of the aloe-wood before my house as a guardian. These are poems from the saying of a man you should emulate — O sons of al-Ashara, cloak yourselves and gird yourselves.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: follows as an example, emulates, copies
the Samhari spear (attributed to Samhar, a renowned spear-maker)
the clamor, the rabble, the tumult
by his oath of protection, by his covenant
a thick-trunked tree burned to produce smoke signals (aloe or similar)
a guardian, a defender (one who wards off)

context: A short poem in which Antara invites Banu al-Ashara to model themselves after him, presenting himself as superior to their own father in honor and courage, and warning that his power will reach them even from a distance.

titleAr: كأن رماحهم أشطان بئر لها في كل مدلجة خدودتركت
titleEn: Their Spears Like Well-Ropes in Every Dark Path

verses.sadr: كأن رماحهم أشطان بئر لها في كل مدلجة خدودتركت
verses.ajuz: بني الهجيم لهم دوار إذا تمضي جماعتهم تعود

translationEn: Their spears are like the ropes of a well that has furrows in every dark night-path. I left Banu al-Hujam with a spinning dizziness — whenever their group passes they return again. I left Juriya the Umari a sound middle with balanced and firm purpose. If he recovers, I blew no charm over him; if he is lost, then his loss is his just due. Does Juriya know that the quiver of our arrows is filled by the brave and noble warrior? When the spears fell upon his shoulders, he turned away crouching, with aversion. Their spears are like the ropes of a well that has furrows in every dark night-path.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: ropes of a well (the long ropes used to lower buckets)
a dark road traveled at night, a path in darkness
furrows, grooves worn by ropes (also: cheeks)
dizziness, vertigo (from the spinning of confusion in battle)
its quiver (jafir: a large quiver for arrows)

context: A brief war poem describing a raid on Banu al-Hujam, featuring Antara's characteristic vivid simile of spears as well-ropes — both long, pliant, and capable of drawing things up (or down, to death).

titleAr: إذا قيل من للمعضلات أجابه عظام اللهى منا طوال
titleEn: When Asked Who Answers Hardships

verses.sadr: إذا قيل من للمعضلات أجابه عظام اللهى منا طوال
verses.ajuz: السواعدوللموت خير للفتى من حياته إذا لم يثب للأمر إلا بقائد
على الحي منا كل أروع ماجد
لما نال من معروفها غير زاهد
ولا عند خير إن رجاه بواحد

translationEn: When it is asked who answers the hardships, the great-jawed, long-armed ones among us respond. Death is better for a young man than his life when he cannot rise to a matter except by a leader's guidance. So tackle great matters yourself and do not be faint-hearted in resolve, leaving tasks for the worthless. When the wind brings heavy rainless clouds driven along like pursued she-camels in the night. It is followed by the declining stars of those who retreat, bringing dust and cold sparse rain in the night. Our great and generous men suffice the needs of guests until they lead them to rest upon our encampment. You see them resolving and binding matters, not indifferent to the goodness they have attained. And our brother is not a coward when evil threatens, nor standing alone when good is hoped for.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the great-jawed (lavish in generosity, with big jawbones as sign of strength)
faint of heart, struck with terror at the heart
worthless, lowly men (pl. of سائد or سودة)
clouds carrying no rain, heavy rainless clouds
pursued young she-camels (simile for driven clouds)
noble, generous, magnificent (one who fills the heart with wonder)

context: A poem extolling the virtues of Antara's people — their generosity to guests, their courage, their readiness to tackle hardship — combined with his philosophy that a man who cannot act without a leader's push is better off dead.

titleAr: أقمت الحق في الهندي رغما وأظهرت الضلال من
titleEn: I Established Right with the Indian Sword Forcibly

verses.sadr: أقمت الحق في الهندي رغما وأظهرت الضلال من
verses.ajuz: الرشادإذا جحد الجميل بنو قراد وجازى بالقبيح بنو زياد
إذا ما الصخر كر على الزناد

verses.sadr: وكم داع دعا في الحرب باسمي

translationEn: I established right with the Indian sword by force, and made error manifest from right guidance — when Banu Qurrad denied my favor and Banu Ziyad repaid goodness with evil. Yet they are the lords of Abs wherever they settle, so they claim, and the knights of the land. I bear no fault or blame if I set right my condition by force where corruption prevailed. For fire blazes in solid rock when flint strikes against the steel. Union is hoped for after abandonment sometimes, just as closeness is hoped for after distance. I showed forbearance but you did not acknowledge its worth, nor did your tribe recall my affection. After this forbearance I shall act in ignorance until I shed the blood of settled folk and desert nomads. The sword will complain from my hand of weariness and my shoulder will tire of bearing the sword-belt. You witnessed on the day of Tayy my deeds with sharp Indian swords. I drove the horses back empty and bewildered, and herded their chargers while the sword was the driver. If the spear-tip had a tongue it would tell how many a coat of mail it pierced reaching the heart. How many a caller called my name in battle, and I penetrated the belly of the one who called. The answer comes in word and deed with white Indian blades and tall brown spears. So be wary of it, O Amr, and do not fill your eyelids with sleep.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the Indian sword (Indian steel was renowned for its quality)
by force, against one's will, forcibly
the flint-and-steel fire-striker, a fire-making kit
the sword-belt, the strap from which a sword is hung
the sharp Indian swords (pl. of مهند حاد)
tall upright spears (pl. of أصعد / صعود)
the belly, the viscera, the inner organs

context: Antara confronts Banu Qurrad and Banu Ziyad — who he claims are ungrateful for his protection — warning that his patience has limits and threatening devastating retaliation. He warns Amr specifically to stay vigilant.

titleAr: وتسهر لي أعين الحاسدين وترقد أعين أهل
titleEn: The Eyes of the Envious Stay Sleepless for Me

verses.sadr: وتسهر لي أعين الحاسدين وترقد أعين أهل
verses.ajuz: الودادأرض الشربة شعب ووادي رحلت وأهلها في فؤادي

translationEn: The eyes of the envious stay sleepless because of me, while the eyes of the devoted rest in sleep. The land of al-Sharba — its valley and ravine — I have left, but its people live in my heart. They dwell in it and in my sight even though they are far in the blackness of distance. When lightning flashes from their encampment I lie awake and spend the night allied to sleeplessness. The scent of the lavender reminds my nose of the breezes of maidens and those of gracious gifts. O Abla, bestow on me the phantom of imagination upon the longing one and the sweetness of sleep. Perhaps a single glance from you could revive the last flicker of life in one slain by estrangement and distance. By your right, the horse's back has never ceased to be my resting place, my sword and coat of mail my pillow — until I trample the lands of Iraq and destroy its settled towns and desert regions. When the market of souls for sale is held and the crier calls and announces within it, and the horses come under the dust with the clash of spears and the blows of iron — there I will ram their horsemen until they return humiliated like pillars. And I return with the she-camels laden heavily, moving gently while Shaybuub the horse drives them.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the envious ones, those consumed by envy (pl. of حاسد)
a tiny remaining spark of life, a last flicker of soul
the name of Antara's famous black horse

context: A poem combining love longing for Abla with the warrior's vow to trample Iraq in her honor. The mention of Shaybuub — Antara's legendary black horse — is one of the poem's most recognizable details.

titleAr: وأما القائلون قتيل طعن فذلك مصرع البطل
titleEn: And Those Who Say He Was Slain by a Thrust

verses.sadr: وأما القائلون قتيل طعن فذلك مصرع البطل
verses.ajuz: الجليدألا من مبلغ أهل الجحود مقال فتى وفي بالعهود

verses.sadr: إذا ما الحرب دارت لي رحاها

translationEn: Those who say he was slain by a thrust — that is the death-place of the resolute hero. Is there anyone to convey to the ungrateful the words of a young man faithful to his covenants? I shall go out to single combat free of worry, with a heart forged from iron ingots. I shall thrust with the spear until my enemy sees me like a spark from afar. When the millstone of war turns for me and death becomes sweet for the mighty man — you see white blades flashing in its blazing, adhering to the sinews of the forearms. I drive them forward, but with men whose hearts are like the stone of the flat desert. And horses trained to wade through death that would age the crown of a newborn child. I shall charge with the black horse against the black enemy, and dye my forearm in the blood of the lion. In a kingdom upon which rests the crown of glory, with men of Banu Abs as witnesses. As for those who say: lion of his people — that is true pride, not the glory of ancestors. As for those who say: slain by a thrust — that is the death-place of the resolute hero.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the resolute hero, the bold champion (جليد: firm, steadfast)
the ungrateful people, those who deny favors
ingots of iron, iron pieces (pl. of زبرة)
blazing fire, the tongue of flame
the crown of a newborn child (idiom: what would make even a baby go grey)
a lion, used as a title for a great warrior

context: Antara anticipates his own death in battle and embraces it as the only fitting end for a warrior. He contrasts false pride in ancestry with true pride earned through deeds, in a poem that serves almost as a warrior's testament.

titleAr: وعدنا بالنهاب وبالسرايا وبالأسرى تكبل بالصفادصحا
titleEn: We Returned with Plunder and Raiding Parties

verses.sadr: وعدنا بالنهاب وبالسرايا وبالأسرى تكبل بالصفادصحا
verses.ajuz: من بعد سكرته فؤادي وعاود مقلتي طيب الرقاد
إذا ما لج قومك في بعادي

verses.sadr: غدوا لما رأوا من حد سيفي

translationEn: We returned with plunder and raiding parties and with prisoners shackled in iron fetters. My heart sobered after its intoxication and sweet sleep returned to my eyelids. He who opposes me became humiliated, burdened with worry, with no one to ransom him. He sees in his sleep the ravages of my sword and complains of what he sees to his pillow. O Abla, you have witnessed my deeds and the difference between error and right guidance has become clear to you. If you see someone like me, then abandon me — let no shame from my dark skin reach you. But if not, then remember my thrusting and my striking when your people persist in keeping me away. I struck the settlements of Kinda while they thundered like thunder from the galloping of chargers. I scattered the horsemen in its highlands with thrusting like the mouths of water-skins. And Khath'am — we gave them a morning visit at dawn before the crier called. They fled when they saw from the edge of my sword the herald of death driving sharp through souls. We returned with plunder and raiding parties and with prisoners shackled in iron fetters.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: plunder, booty (the spoils of a raid)
iron fetters, shackles for prisoners (pl. of صفد)
the ravages of my sword, its sudden lethal strikes
the pillow (complaining to a pillow: idiom for powerless distress)
water-skins (pl. of مزادة); wounds compared to gaping mouths
we gave them a morning raid (the dawn attack was the classic form of surprise)

context: Antara recounts successful raids against Kinda and Khath'am, returning with prisoners and plunder, while also addressing Abla — daring her to reject him for his dark skin, or else honor his valor.

titleAr: إذا عادت بنو الأعجام تهوي وقد ولت ونكست البنوداألا
titleEn: When the Foreigners Return Falling in Defeat

verses.sadr: إذا عادت بنو الأعجام تهوي وقد ولت ونكست البنوداألا
verses.ajuz: يا عبل ضيعت العهودا وأمسى حبك الماضي صدودا

translationEn: When the foreigners return falling, their banners turned and lowered — O Abla, you have wasted the covenants and your former love has become aversion. Youth has not passed, we have not grown old, and time has not worn out what was new for us. Our sharp swords have not ceased being keen — our fingertips cut through iron with them. Ask the Fazarites about us when we satisfied our hearts from their horsemen. We left their women bewildered at pre-dawn, slapping their cheeks. We filled all the lands with terror so that all peoples became as slaves to us. We surpassed the Pleiades in glory and left no delegations for those who sought to reach us. When a boy of ours reaches the age of weaning, our enemies prostrate before him. Whoever approaches us with a great calamity in mind will find among us black lions. On the day of giving we bestow all we possess and fill the earth with beneficence and generosity. In every war we shoe our horses with the bleeding bones or skins of enemies. Is there anyone to convey to al-Nu'man from us a message that will reach him soundly — when the foreigners return falling, their banners turned and lowered?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: hearts/livers of warriors (pl. of كبد — satisfied their rage)
weaning (age at which a child is weaned from milk)
giving generously, the day of bestowal
we shoe our horses — here with the bones of enemies (hyperbole of massacre)

context: A grand boast addressed to al-Nu'man alongside lament for Abla's broken covenants. The poem reaches hyperbolic heights: enemies prostrating before weaned infants and horses shod with enemy bones — classic pre-Islamic fakhr excess.

titleAr: ولولا صارمي وسنان رمحي لما رفعت بنو عبس
titleEn: Were It Not for My Sword and My Spear-Tip

verses.sadr: ولولا صارمي وسنان رمحي لما رفعت بنو عبس
verses.ajuz: عماداأعادي صرف دهر لا يعادى وأحتمل القطيعة والبعادا

verses.sadr: سلي يا عبل قومك عن فعالي

translationEn: Were it not for my sharp sword and the tip of my spear, Banu Abs would never have raised a tent-pole. I oppose the reversals of a time that cannot be opposed, and I bear estrangement and distance. I openly show loyalty to a people who have abandoned me, even though their hearts have betrayed affection. I console a sick heart with hopes and with beautiful patience, however long it lasts. The enemies taunt me for the darkness of my skin — but the whiteness of my virtues wipes away the darkness. Ask your people, O Abla, about my deeds and those who witnessed the battle-encounter and the pursuit. I entered war while heroes around me shook their hands with tall brown spears. I plunged with my very soul into the sea of death while the fire of war blazed fiercely. I returned dyed in the blood of enemies, and the exertion of the chase had dyed the horse's neck. How many a voluptuous virgin girl I left behind, whose weeping voice grieves the heart. My sword is two-edged and keen — its blade edges split solid rock. My spear — whenever I thrust it into a pierced one, he could never again see with his eyes the path of right guidance. Were it not for my sharp sword and the tip of my spear, Banu Abs would never have raised a tent-pole.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: my sharp sword (من الصارم: the cutting one)
the tip of my spear, the spear-head
a tent-pole, a central pillar (idiom: without which nothing stands)
my virtues, my noble traits (pl. of خصيلة)
a voluptuous young virgin woman (raddah: full and heavy)
two-edged and keen (marhaf: sharpened to a fine edge)
the horse's neck and upper chest (not generosity in this context)

context: Antara states his essential paradox plainly: the tribe that diminishes him, mocks his skin, and neglects him stands only because of his sword and spear. The poem is a dignified complaint combined with defiant pride.

titleAr: بهاليل مثل الأسد في كل موطن كأن دم الأعداء في فمهم
titleEn: Noble Men Like Lions in Every Station

verses.sadr: بهاليل مثل الأسد في كل موطن كأن دم الأعداء في فمهم
verses.ajuz: شهدلأي حبيب يحسن الرأي والود وأكثر هذا الناس ليس لهم عهد

verses.sadr: أريد من الأيام ما لا يضرها

verses.ajuz: وصال ولا يلهيه من حله عقد
وأين العلا إن لم يساعدني الجد

verses.sadr: فيالك من قلب توقد في الحشا
verses.ajuz: ويالك من دمع غزير له مد
فلي بين أضلاعي لها أسد ورد

verses.sadr: إذا كان لا يمضي الحسام ينفسه
يسر الفتى دهر وقد كان ساءه
ولا مال إلا ما أفادك نيله
verses.ajuz: ثناء ولا مال لمن لاله مجد

verses.sadr: ولا عاش إلا من يصاحب فتية

verses.ajuz: وإن ندبوا يوما إلى غارة جدوا
يروح إلى ظعن القبائل أو يغدو

verses.sadr: خفيت على إثر الطريدة في الفلا
بهاليل مثل الأسد في كل موطن
verses.ajuz: كأن دم الأعداء في فمهم شهد

translationEn: Noble generous men like lions in every station, as if the blood of enemies in their mouths were honey. For which beloved is good counsel and affection appropriate — when most of this humanity has no covenant? I want from the days what does not harm them — but will intense effort ward off their calamities from me? This world does not obey us, and no creature can do without humoring it. The servants and slaves belong to the incapable, while the solitary hero serves himself in it. Every near relation of mine is far in affection, and every friend between his ribs harbors a grudge. By God, what a heart — its sick one is not cured by union, and no knot distracts it from what it has undone. It compels me to seek glory through the spear — but where is loftiness if earnest effort does not aid me? I love as my spear and my sword love — and a full coat of supple mail and a firm coat of mail. What a heart that blazes in the chest! And what a copious tear with its flowing tide! If the days reveal every great calamity, within my ribs I have a returning lion against them. If the sword does not cut by itself, then for the one who strikes well with its hilt there is an edge. Around me — rather than among all people — is a band whose affection is hidden while its rancor shows. Time gladdened a young man who it had once grieved — and the days serve him while he is their slave. There is no wealth except what brings you praise by its attainment — and no wealth for one who has no glory. Only he truly lives who accompanies generous bold youths who are heedless of ill omen and good fortune. When they sought the raid they girded themselves, and when summoned to a foray one day they were earnest. Would I but know whether hopes will reach me and bring me to the enemies on a swimming horse that runs. A generous steed — when its chest cleaves through the gatherings, it goes out in the evening or the morning toward the departing women of the tribes. I concealed myself on the track of the pursued prey in the wilderness when the scorching ground blazed and the hunters went different ways. Banu Abs accompanied me — a band whose nobility extends among the tribes: noble generous men like lions in every station, as if the blood of enemies in their mouths were honey.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: noble generous men, great lords (pl. of بهلول)
honey (as sweet as honey — the blood of enemies is their joy)
noble generous bold men (pl. of غطريف)
ill omen and good fortune (they ignore both, caring only for glory)
a swimming (fast-moving) horse, one that glides like it swims
the pursued prey, the quarry of a hunt or raid

context: The closing poem of the first batch, a reflective meditation on the treachery of time, the ingratitude of companions, and the longing for the right horse and right companions to carry Antara to glory. It ends with the same image it opened with — noble men whose joy in enemies' blood is as sweet as honey.

titleEn: Rise to Take Vengeance

verses.sadr: هل عيشة طابت لنا إلا وقد

verses.ajuz: يا لهف نفسي إذ رأت توسيدها

translationEn: Rise without hesitation to take vengeance until you annihilate the multitudes of your enemies. Time's calamities have exceeded their bounds and exhausted all their effort, decreeing death upon us and exchanging the white nights for black. The beloved ones turned away and sought separation. The dead have settled into their graves as the days wove their shrouds and spring adorned their tombs. Every joy we tasted was worn away by time, and every edifice of glory was ultimately toppled by fate. O Qays, the fire in our chests blazes — rise to avenge what we have lost.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: fuel, that which feeds a fire

context: This poem is a call to vengeance addressed to Qays, combining elegiac reflection on the cruelty of time and death with a martial exhortation to rise and fight. It is characteristic of Antara's ability to weave themes of mortality and loss with his warrior ethos.

titleEn: Tell Husn of My Enmity

verses.ajuz: يبيت على نار من الحزن والوجد

verses.sadr: إذا فاض دمعي واستهل على خدي

verses.ajuz: وطال المدى ماذا يلاقون من بعدي
أخاف الأعادي أو أذل من الطرد
إذا اهتز قلب الضد يخفق كالرعد

verses.sadr: متى سل في كفي بيوم كريهة
verses.ajuz: فلا فرق ما بين المشايخ والمرد

verses.sadr: وما الفخر إلا أن تكون عمامتي

verses.ajuz: فلا تذكرا أطلال سلمى ولا هند

verses.sadr: ولا تذكرا لي غير خيل مغيرة

verses.ajuz: نشقت له ريحا ألذ من الند

verses.sadr: ولي من حسامي كل يوم على الثرى
verses.ajuz: نقوش دم تغني الندامى عن الورد
إذا كان في يوم الوغى قاطع الحد

translationEn: Tell Husn that whoever nurses enmity against me will spend his nights burning with grief and longing. When my tears flow, I remember my people's injustice — how I built their glory with my sword yet they tore down mine, blaming my dark skin while their own deeds are blacker than my complexion. My sword asks no distinction between elder and youth on the day of battle, and my only pride is the Indian blade wrapped in my turban. My companions, when you sober up, speak not of ruined campsites but of charging horses and the perfume of battle-dust. O Fazara, you have roused a lion of the thicket who knows neither error nor right guidance — only the sword.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the Indian (wootz) sword, famed for sharpness
the worn-out sheath — age of the sword's scabbard

context: This poem is a defiant address to Husn (likely Husn ibn Damdam, an enemy of Antara) and to his own tribe of Fazara, asserting his warrior pride and responding to those who mock his African descent. The famous line equating black skin to dark deeds is one of Antara's most celebrated retorts.

titleEn: Time Struck Me Like a Traitor

verses.sadr: يا دهر لا تبق علي فقد دنا
verses.ajuz: ما كنت أطلب قبل ذا وأريد

verses.sadr: فالقتل لي من بعد عبلة راحة
يا عبل! قد دنت المنية فاندبي
verses.ajuz: يا عبل! إن تبكي علي فقد

verses.sadr: بكى صرف الزمان علي وهو حسود
ففعائلي في كل يوم ذكرهن جديد
ولقد لقيت الفرس يا ابنة مالك
يا عبل كم من جحفل فرقته

translationEn: Time has struck me with the blow of a treacherous foe, now miserly, now generous. The pride of men is their chains and shackles, just as women's pride lies in their necklaces and ornaments. O Time, leave me be — death is now a relief now that Abla has departed, for life without her is cursed misery. O Abla, if they shed my blood, my deeds will be remembered every day anew. I met the Persian armies, their hosts filling the plains, surging like a sea — yet they found lions armored in iron. We let the swords judge between us, and spear-tips bore witness.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a violent blow, an overpowering assault
the blows/turns of time, fate's reversals
a vast army, a great host

context: A poem of deep personal anguish in which Antara laments the separation from Abla and reflects on the cruelty of fate. The mention of facing Persian armies provides the warrior backdrop against which his vulnerable longing for his beloved is set.

titleAr: ألا قاتل الله الهوى كم بسيفه
titleEn: Curse the Sword of Love

verses.sadr: ألا قاتل الله الهوى كم بسيفه
verses.ajuz: قتيل غرام لا يوسد في اللحد

verses.sadr: إذا رشقت قلبي سهام من الصد
لبست لها درعا من الصبر مانعا
وبت بطيف منك يا عبل قانعا
verses.ajuz: ولو بات يسرى في الظلام على خدي
على كبد حرى تذوب من الوجد

verses.sadr: ويا برق إن عرضت من جانب الحمى
verses.ajuz: فحي بني عبس على العلم السعدي
فكن أنت في أكنافها نير الوقد

verses.sadr: عدمت اللقا إن كنت بعد فراقها
وما شاق قلبي في الدجى غير طائر
verses.ajuz: ينوح على غصن رطيب من الرند

verses.sadr: به مثل ما بي فهو يخفى من الجوى
verses.ajuz: كمثل الذي أخفي ويبدي إلي أبدي

translationEn: Curse love and how many of its victims lie slain, unwept in their graves. When arrows of rejection struck my heart and time exchanged nearness for distance, I donned the armor of patience and faced the army of longing alone. I spend nights content with your phantom, O Abla, though it is only your apparition gliding through darkness. I beseech the Hijaz wind to breathe upon my burning liver melting with grief. O lightning, if you pass near the sanctuary, greet the sons of Abs at the landmark of Sa'd. Let me be deprived of reunion if I ever sleep without her image before me. Only a bird in the darkness stirs my heart, mourning on a green branch of rend-tree, suffering as I do.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to be laid on a pillow; euphemism for burial
turning away, rejection by the beloved
phantom, apparition of the beloved in sleep
a burning liver; seat of passion in Arabic poetry
the bay-laurel tree, associated with Arabia

context: One of Antara's most lyrical ghazal poems, structured around the metaphor of love as a battlefield where the poet fights alone. The poet addresses natural elements — wind and lightning — as messengers to his beloved Abla, a classical convention of Arabic love poetry.

titleEn: I Shall Soon Break Their Captivity

verses.ajuz: ما زاد صقلا جاد يوم جلاد

verses.sadr: وحسام قد كنت من عهد شداد

translationEn: I shall soon break their captivity from the hands of enemies and enviers. The fire of longing and separation has burned me after losing homeland and children. My hair has turned white though it was once jet-black, and I remembered Abla on the day she came to bid farewell, her tears flowing in fear of the distance. I told her: stop weeping, for my heart is already melting with grief. Yet I am like a sword — the more it is polished, the keener it cuts in battle. The calamities of fate have tempered me until they set me on the path of wisdom, and I have routed heroes in every valley, leaving horsemen fallen by my spear.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the very core of the heart

context: This poem opens with a vow of rescue and liberation, then shifts into personal elegy as Antara meditates on aging, separation from Abla, and the grief of exile. The central simile comparing the poet to a sword that grows sharper with polishing is a signature image of his fakhr tradition.

titleEn: I Humbled the Enviers by My Might

verses.sadr: يا مسرح الآرام في وادي الحمى
verses.ajuz: هل فيك ذو شجن يروح ويغتدي

verses.sadr: يا عبل كم يشجى فؤادي بالنوى
ولقد حبست الدمع لا بخلا به
لو كنت مثلي ما لبثت ملونا

verses.ajuz: من كل أروع في الكريهة أصيد

translationEn: I humbled the enviers by my might until they bowed before it in prostration. Between al-Aqiq and Barqat Thuhamd lies a campsite of Abla's, its landmarks worn. O grazing ground of gazelles in the valley of refuge, does any grief-stricken one still pass through you morning and evening? Abla, how the departure pains my heart and the black crow's cry startles me — how can I forget when every mourning dove I hear makes me the first to lament? The beauties raised their tents, faces radiant enough to outshine the Pleiades, their eyes enchanting without kohl. They said the meeting would be tomorrow — and how long tomorrow seems to the lovesick! Then I plunged into unknown wilderness with young warriors of Abs whose spear-tips gleamed like stars in the battle-dust, and the hooves of noble horses thundered like lightning across the plain — and there, the clan of Abs witnessed my stand.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to humiliate someone (lit. press their nose to the ground)
one with a graceful, swaying neck (of gazelles or beauties)
the lovesick wanderer, one overwhelmed by passion
a wide empty plain, barren expanse

context: This is Antara's longest poem in the batch — a grand qasida that opens with a boast of victory over enviers, transitions through an elegiac nasib (love-prelude) mourning Abla's campsite, and closes with an extended and vivid battle description. It is an exemplar of the classical tripartite qasida structure.

titleEn: If My Eyes See the Riding Camels

verses.sadr: إذا الريح هبت من ربى العلم السعدي

verses.ajuz: فما عرفوا قدري ولا حفظوا عهدي
لما اخترت قرب الدار يوما على البعد
إذا كلمت ميتا يقوم من اللحد
تقول: إذا اسود الدجى فاطلعي بعدي

verses.sadr: وقال لها البدر المنير ألا اسفري
verses.ajuz: فإنك مثلي في الكمال وفي السعد
وقد نثرت من خدها رطب الورد
ومن عجب أن يقطع السيف في الغمد
فيغشاه ليل من دجى شعرها الجعد

verses.sadr: فهل تسمح الأيام يا ابنة مالك
verses.ajuz: بوصل يداوي القلب من ألم الصد

verses.sadr: سأحلم عن قومي ولو سفكوا دمي
verses.ajuz: وأجرع فيك الصبر دون الملا وحدي
فها أنتم أشجاكم البعد من بعدي
وقد كان ظني لا أفارقكم جهدي

translationEn: If my eyes behold the riding camels and their riders, I would lay my cheek beneath their hooves. When the wind blows from the hills of the Sa'd landmark, its coolness quenches the burning of longing and grief. A girl dwells in the tents — slender-waisted, whose glance could raise a dead man from his grave. The setting sun pointed to her, saying: When darkness falls, rise after me. The full moon said: Unveil yourself, for you match me in perfection and grace. She turned away in modesty, then dropped her veil, scattering the freshness of roses from her cheek, and drew a sword from her languorous eyelids sharper than her father's blade — a sword that wounds while still sheathed. Her musk-fragrance deepens with every breath, and the dawn's light that breaks beneath her brow is swallowed by the night of her curling hair.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: face veil covering the lower part of the face
swaying gracefully at the sides, supple-figured
the front teeth, used as a site of beauty

context: A richly elaborated ghazal centered on an extended portrait of a beloved woman compared successively to the sun, the moon, a sword, and the dawn. Antara's portrayal here is among the most detailed feminine descriptions in his diwan, blending sensory imagery with mythological hyperbole.

titleAr: حوى كل حسن في الكواعب شخصها
titleEn: Her Form Gathers All Beauty

verses.sadr: حوى كل حسن في الكواعب شخصها

verses.ajuz: إذا أسفرت بدر بدا في المحاشد

verses.sadr: شكت سقما كيما تعاد وما بها
من البيض لا تلقاك إلا مصونة

translationEn: Her form gathers all beauty among young women — the only faults she bears are those invented by the envious. She plays with men's minds, and when she unveils, she shines like a full moon appearing before great assemblies. She feigns illness so that visitors come to her, yet her only ailment is the languor of her eyes. Pure and always modest, she moves like a branch of the moringa tree swaying among handmaids. When the Pleiades appear at evening, they seem to be strung upon her neck as a necklace.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: playful, one who toys with affections
she unveiled, lit up (of face or dawn)
languid drooping of the eyes, considered beautiful
branch of the moringa/ben tree, symbol of graceful movement

context: A concise ghazal of five verses offering a portrait of an unnamed beauty. The brevity and density of imagery — the moon, the Pleiades, the moringa branch — make this an accessible example of Antara's descriptive style.

titleAr: لئن تشمت الأعداء يا بنت مالك
titleEn: Though Enemies Gloat, Daughter of Malik

verses.sadr: لئن تشمت الأعداء يا بنت مالك
إذا كان دمعي شاهدي كيف أجحد
وهيهات يخفى ما أكن من الهوى
إلى الله أشكو جور قومي وظلمهم
verses.ajuz: إذا لم أجد خلا على البعد يعضد

verses.sadr: حرام علي النوم يا ابنة مالك
verses.ajuz: ومن فرشه جمر الغضا كيف يرقد
لعل لهيبي من ثرى الأرض يبرد

verses.sadr: رحلت وقلبي يا ابنة العم تائه

translationEn: Though enemies gloat, O daughter of Malik, my devotion to you remains as it has always been. How can I deny it when my own tears bear witness and the fire of longing blazes in my chest? It is impossible to conceal the love I harbor — my garment of sickness is renewed every day. I fight my yearnings with patience while my heart lies shackled in the chains of love. O my friend, the love of Abla has become my killer, yet my courage is unbroken and my Indian sword still gleams. Sleep is forbidden to me — how can one rest whose bed is burning embers? I shall lament until the birds know of my sorrow and the cooing dove takes pity on me. I kiss the earth where you dwell, hoping its dust cools my burning.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: embers of the ghada tree, known for staying hot long
litters on camels carrying women (pl. of ظعينة)
to feel compassion for, to mourn someone

context: A ghazal of protest and declaration in which Antara insists on his constancy to Abla despite enemies gloating over his suffering. The interplay between warrior identity (the gleaming sword) and lover's vulnerability (the burning bed of embers) is characteristic of his poetic persona.

titleEn: I Met Their Cavalry with My Own
translationEn: I advanced against their cavalry with my own cavalry, riders upon whom lions pounce and rend. O coward — does your tail tremble in fear of me? Come, for here I stand. If ever we meet alone, your haunches will quake and scatter in terror. My sword is keen in the fist of a hand whose tendons show no weakness. My blade is like a lightning flash — never notched, never broken — shining like fire at the spear-tip in the night. You will know which of us is closer to death when the blazing lances are brought near. My weapons are my lineage, and my horse is my companion in every panic.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to seize and rend violently (of lions)
the two upper halves of the tail (of animals)
the sinews/tendons on the back of the hand
a flash of lightning; used metaphorically for a bright sword

context: A combative fakhr poem addressing a specific cowardly enemy, mixing martial boasts with vivid descriptions of weapons and a section on the pastoral world of camels and herders. The boast of encountering enemies alone is a recurrent motif in Antara's self-glorification.

titleEn: You Had No Right to Insult Us
translationEn: You had no right to insult us, O sons of al-Ushra, when the contest of pride came to the fore. Whoever asks about me — know that I own a noble mare that neither roams nor is lent out. She is kept close to the tent in winter, not left behind with the foals. In summer she has grain-stores and she-camels, and six of her best are heavy milkers. I declare this openly to the sons of al-Ushra — the time for secrets is past. I have slain your leaders and cut down your contemptible ones as one culls the weak.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: when the contest/competition of boasting became serious
to roam freely without a master
to be lent to another (of animals)
kept near the tent (an honor for horses)
noble animals, prized she-camels (pl. of كريمة)

context: A short defiant poem addressed to the clan of al-Ushra who insulted Antara. He responds with a declaration of his nobility symbolized through his prized mare and she-camels, then announces openly that he has killed their men.

titleEn: Every Steed Swimming in the Dust
translationEn: Every horse swimming through the dust is like a great eagle spreading its wings when washed by water. A lean wolf-like hound, sleek as a wolf in the dense reeds, guards every frontier we fear.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a horse with smooth flowing movement (as if swimming)
a large eagle with wide-spread wings
a great eagle (the one that breaks/spreads its wings)
lean-bellied, hollow-flanked (of a greyhound or wolf)
a thicket of reeds, dense vegetation

context: A short two-verse fragment of vivid nature and animal description. Horses are compared to soaring eagles and guardian greyhounds to wolves — a compact example of Antara's descriptive animal imagery.

titleAr: إن عشت فهي التي ما عشت مالكتي
titleEn: If I Live She Owns Me, If I Die

verses.sadr: إن عشت فهي التي ما عشت مالكتي
ولا أرى مؤنسا غير الحسام وإن
verses.ajuz: قل الأعادي غداة الروع أو كثروا

verses.sadr: فحاذري يا سباع البر من رجل
