verses.ajuz: إذا انتضى سيفه لا ينفع الحذر

verses.sadr: ما خالد بعدما قد سرت طالبه
يا عبل يهنئك ما يأتيك من نعم
يا من رمت مهجتي من نبل مقلتها

verses.ajuz: ونار هجرك لا تبقي ولا تذر

verses.sadr: كم ليلة قد قطعنا فيك صالحة

translationEn: If I live, she owns me for all my life; if I die, nights will claim their due. I traverse deserts in the dark of night, crossing wilderness and scorching sands with only my sword for companion. Beware of me, wild beasts — when I draw my blade, no caution avails. O Abla, the joy of your union is an adorned garden, and the fire of your abandonment leaves nothing standing. I invoke blessing on the Sa'd landmark — how many fine nights did we spend there, drinking wine bright as fire's flame, served by a slender Arab girl with eyes of pure white and dark iris.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: skulls split open (grim fruit of battle)
abundant, easy, pleasant (of life or provisions)
the beautiful contrast of white and dark in the eyes
the landmark/mountain of Sa'd, associated with Abla's homeland

context: A moving poem balanced between the warrior's lonely desert journeys and tender memory of nights spent with companions near Abla's homeland. The opening declaration of unconditional devotion — alive or dead — is one of the most quoted lines from Antara's ghazal corpus.

titleEn: How Can I Bear Patience from My Beloved

verses.ajuz: وقد أضرمت نار الهوى في أضالعي

verses.sadr: سقى الله عمي من يد الموت جرعة
كما قاد مثلي بالمحال إلى الردى

verses.ajuz: إذا غبت عنا في القفار الشواسع

verses.sadr: وحقك لا حاولت في الدهر سلوة

verses.ajuz: وعش ناعما في غبطة غير جازع

verses.sadr: فقلت لها: يا عبل إني مسافر
خلقنا لهذا الحب من قبل يومنا
أيا علم السعدي هل أنا راجع
أيا صادحات الأيك إن مت فاندبي
ونوحي على من مات ظلما ولم ينل
ويا خيل فابكي فارسا كان يلتقي

verses.ajuz: وقد شاع ذكري في جميع المجامع
عن اللوم إن اللوم ليس بنافع

translationEn: How can I endure patience from the one I love when the fire of passion blazes in my ribs? The eyelids of veiled maidens are sharper than the thinnest cutting blades — when bared they humble the bravest warrior. May my uncle taste death for what he has done, leading a man like me to ruin through impossible demands and dangling my hopes by the hem of ambitions. Abla bade me farewell as if certain I would not return, weeping and asking how I would fare in the distant wastes. I swore by her that I would never seek consolation and nothing would alter my love. O Sa'd's landmark, shall I ever return to gaze upon your horizons? O morning breeze of the moringa, tell Abla where my camp now lies. O singing birds of the thicket, if I die, mourn upon my grave. O my horses, weep for a rider who once faced the forefront of death in the battle-dust but now lies far away in passion's chains.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: ribs (pl. of ضلع), seat of the heart and emotions
the eye-sockets, the area around the eyes
singing birds of the thicket (nightingales, doves)
the roar and chaos of battle
bonds, fetters; things that follow and bind

context: One of the most emotionally complex poems in Antara's diwan, interweaving bitter reproach of his uncle (who demanded an impossible bride-price), tender dialogue with Abla at parting, and a sustained apostrophe to natural elements — wind, lightning, birds, horses — asking them to carry his messages and mourn his fate.

titleEn: I Shall Seek Him in Your Land
translationEn: I shall seek him out in your own land and repay him for what he has done. O Abu al-Yaqzan, greed has led you astray — you will meet a horseman who cannot be turned back. You visited me seeking a moment of inattention, as the wolf visits the grazing sheep. O Abu al-Yaqzan, how often has the prey escaped while the hunter fell? If you complain of love's pains, I will cure you with this sword of mine that cuts however it swings. I am the Black One, the slave who charges into the cavalry when the battle-dust rises. My lineage is my sword and my spear — they are my companions when terror intensifies. O sons of Shayban, my uncle has wronged you and today his injustice returns upon you — he led Bustam to his death, clinging to the tail of his own greed.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one who cannot be repelled or turned back
the dust of battle (raised by charging horses)
the hem/tail of greed (metaphor for being led by greed)
place of death, place of downfall

context: A poem of personal menace addressed to Abu al-Yaqzan, an enemy. Antara uses the famous self-identification 'I am the Black One, the slave' as an act of pride rather than shame, subverting social stigma by claiming his dark skin and enslaved origins as marks of his exceptional martial identity.

titleEn: I Left Ruins Where a Company Dwelt

verses.sadr: ما دست في أرض العداة غدوة
يا عبل كم تنعق غربان الفلا
verses.ajuz: قد مل قلبي في الدجى سماعها

translationEn: I departed from ruins where a company of people had cut off all my hopes of companionship. Fate stretched its arm against me and made war upon me — then saw what struck terror into it. O calamities of time, settle down and sleep, for my determination has torn away your veil. Every morning I set foot in the enemies' land, my sword irrigated its plains with streams of blood. Woe to Shayban when I raid them at dawn and send my bright blades flashing. My spear plunged into their midst and pierced through armor and ribs alike. Their women became mourners lamenting men whose final agonies they now bewail. O Abla, the ravens of the plain never stop croaking — my heart is weary of hearing them in the night.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: ruins of a former dwelling, traces of a camp
a close group, a company of people
her veil (here: fate's disguise being stripped away)
women wailing and mourning the dead
to caw (of ravens/crows), an omen of separation

context: A poem that opens with the motif of deserted ruins but quickly transforms into a warrior's threat against the tribe of Shayban. The closing verse addressing Abla anchors the poem in the personal register, showing how Antara's fakhr and ghazal themes are inseparable.

titleEn: One Like Me Does Not Flinch

verses.ajuz: علوت ولم أجد في الجو ساع

translationEn: One like me does not flinch before the fire of battle, nor do I fall short when called. Abla looked at me when my flowing locks shone like rays of light and exclaimed: By God, what a hero — lions of the lowlands bow before your terror. I told her: Ask the champions about me when the fearful flee the clash of swords. I am the slave whose fortune and glory surpass the Pleiades in height. I climbed to the very sky of glory until I ascended and found no one above me in the heavens. Another tried to follow my path, striving with all his earnestness to catch up — but fell short in the heights while his hands tired of the attempt. A noble horse carries my armor, and in my hand a polished sharp blade cures headaches — and my Samhari spear bears a tip that glimmers like fire on a hilltop.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one who cries out and trembles in pain or fear
blazing fire, the intense heat of battle
the parting of the flowing hair (mark of a hero)
the clash of swords, fierce combat
mourning cries; places of wailing (left by the defeated)
a type of lance, very stiff and straight, from Samhar

context: A poem in which Abla's admiring exclamation provides the dramatic center — she praises Antara directly and he responds with a proud self-description. This structure, where the beloved becomes the witness and affirmer of the warrior's glory, is a distinctive feature of Antara's integration of ghazal and fakhr.

titleEn: I Drove My Sword Through Souls

verses.ajuz: حللا إذا ما الأرض فاح ربيعها

verses.sadr: يا عبل! لا تخشي علي من العدى
وغدا يمر على الأعاجم من يدي
يا عبل! لو أن المنية صورت

translationEn: I drove my sword through souls to annihilate those who would not answer its call and submit. Stand at the dwellings — if their ruins grieve you, perhaps your eyes will pour their tears. Ask where the litters have gone, where the fathers led them, and when they will return. Abla's home is now far from you — since it went distant, sleep has left your eyes. I blessed the land of al-Shurba with morning rains to water its soil, and spring clothed its hills in blooms. O Abla, fear not for me when enemies gather in their multitudes — death itself is a great tree and I am its root and branches. Tomorrow my hand will pass the Persians a cup more bitter than poison, and I shall make their leaders bow to my spear's thrust until even the nursing infant's hair turns white with terror. If Death itself were given form, it would prostrate itself before me.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to place oneself in the midst, to drive through the center
camel-litters carrying women; the departing caravans
a single rain-cloud, a blessed downpour
a deadly poison; also the hot desert wind
belonging to Khosrow (the Persian king), i.e., the Persian armies
ribs — here: what the chest conceals within
a nursing infant — hyperbole for the terror of battle

context: A poem that combines a nasib (elegy for deserted ruins) with boasts against Persian armies. The declaration that 'death itself is a tree and I am its root' is among Antara's most memorable self-mythologizing metaphors, presenting him as the very substance and instrument of mortality.

titleEn: When Heroes Flee My Might
translationEn: When heroes flee in terror of my might, the very horizons seem a cubit or forearm in their shrunken fear. When fate unveils its face to you and time extends its arm in your direction, do not fear death — meet it and resist as best you can. Do not choose a silk bed to die upon; do not weep over ruins and places. If a physician offers to cure you, he is deceived — if doctors knew a cure for death, none would suffer its final agony. At the Day of al-Masani, our deeds spread our fame everywhere. We set up the market of war with our spears, trading souls as merchandise. My horse was the broker of death, wading into its dust buying and selling. My sword was the physician of battle, healing headaches by removing the head. I am the slave you have heard of — now you see me with your own eyes, so you need no more hearsay. I filled the earth with fear of my sword, leaving no room for my foe.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the cardinal directions, the expanses of the earth
face-veil coverings worn over the veil
the death-agony, the final struggle of the dying
the broker of death — the horse personified as a market-agent
the battle, the clash of war (poetic)

context: A poem of pure fakhr that opens with the image of fleeing heroes making the earth seem small, then moves into philosophical advice on facing death, followed by boasts about the battle of al-Masani. The black humor of calling Antara's sword 'the physician who cures headaches' is a characteristic sample of his martial wit.

titleEn: She Bore It with Knowing Patience
translationEn: She endured it with a knowing patience, as a freeborn woman stands firm when the coward's soul flees in panic. The piebald crow ran between them, tattered of wing, its beak like a pair of scissors eager for news — I drove it away so it would never nest again, destined to wail alone. Those whose parting was foretold to me have kept me awake entire nights in pain. A swift devastating raid with its battalions swept through, riders bareheaded and helmeted among them — I turned it away from the women of Amir's clan. And I understood: when my death comes for me, not even the swiftest flight can save me from it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to stand firm, to be anchored (like a ship)
to leap up and flee, to rush away in panic
the piebald (pied) crow, omen of separation
scissors or shears (dual), used as a simile for the crow's beak
a mother bird unable to move who feeds her fledgling in the nest
a devastating swift raid, chaotic assault
fully helmeted, armored to the head

context: An unusual poem in Antara's corpus that features a woman as its central figure of endurance alongside an extended bird-omen description. The piebald crow heralding separation is a standard image in pre-Islamic poetry, but Antara's drive to ward it off gives the passage a distinctive energy.

titleEn: Another I Dragged Along My Spear
translationEn: Another enemy I dragged on my spear — the broad-headed spear-tip buried in Bajali. Had you met me while I wore my armor, you would have learned why armor is worth bearing. I left Jubaylah ibn Abi Adi soaking his garments with the dark flowing blood that clung to him.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to drag the spear through an enemy, leaving it lodged in the wound
a sharpened blade, one that has been honed
a coat of mail, chainmail armor

context: A brief but fierce three-verse fragment cataloguing specific combat kills. The mention of Jubaylah ibn Abi Adi soaked in blood is a typical example of Antara's naming of defeated enemies as a form of boast and historical record.

titleEn: Gleaming Battalions Above All Others

verses.sadr: ألا هل أتاها أن يوم عراعر
verses.ajuz: شفى سقما لو كانت النفس تشتفى

verses.sadr: فجئنا على عمياء ما جمعوا لنا

verses.ajuz: على ظهر مقصي من الأمر محصف

verses.sadr: فإن يك عز في قضاعة ثابت

translationEn: Gleaming battalions, over each one a banner like the shadow of a wide-winged bird in flight. Has news not reached her that the Day of Arayr healed a sickness no soul could otherwise cure? We came upon them blindly — they had assembled a disorganized, leaderless mass against us. They doubted our approach while still plastering their water-troughs, caught unaware on the back of one kept ignorant of affairs. They had no warning until we overwhelmed their homes like the sudden descent of lethal hidden rain. All day we kept charging with Mushrafiya swords and supple Samhari lances, returning to battle again and again while wounds had yet to crust. We refused to give our enemy an inch of equal ground. If the tribe of Qudha'a boasts of its glory at Rahrahar and Asqaf, we have our answer there.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Yemeni swords of the finest quality
a second charge or return to battle after the first
wounds on men and horses from battle
a bow that hums (makes a sound) when the string is drawn
from Radwa, a mountain in Arabia known for its wood for bows

context: A battle poem recounting the Day of Arayr, one of the famous battle-days of the pre-Islamic Arabs. The detail of enemies being caught while still plastering water-troughs vividly depicts the surprise of the raid, and the closing challenge to Qudha'a situates the poem within tribal rivalry.

titleAr: وإن يعيبوا سوادا قد كسيت به
titleEn: If They Fault My Darkness

verses.sadr: وإن يعيبوا سوادا قد كسيت به
يا عبل قري بوادي الرمل آمنة
verses.ajuz: من العداة وإن خوفت لا تخفي

verses.sadr: خافوا من الحرب لما أبصروا فرسي
verses.ajuz: تحت العجاجة يهوي بي إلى التلف

verses.sadr: ثم اقتفوا أثري من بعد ما
verses.ajuz: علموا أن المنية سهم غير منصرف

verses.sadr: ما زلت أنصف خصمي وهو يظلمني
verses.ajuz: حتى غدا من حسامي غير منتصف

translationEn: If they fault the darkness with which I am clothed, know that pearls are hidden in the darkness of their oyster shells. O Abla, rest safely in the valley of sand — fear not your enemies, and do not be frightened even when threats come. Before your tent stand lions whose fingertips carry white blades that cleave helmets and shields. By God, the tribe of Abs has attained all glory and reached the pinnacle of honor. They feared the war when they saw my horse plunging through the dust carrying me toward destruction — then they followed in my wake when they understood that death is an arrow that never turns aside. I waded into the dust on my jet-black horse until it returned dyed in blood and gore. I was always fair to my enemy even as he wronged me — until my sword gave him no further claim to fairness.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: pearls — compared to Antara's inner worth hidden by dark skin
the oyster shell that conceals the pearl
shields made of hide (pl. of حجفة)
jet-black (of extreme darkness, said of a horse)
one who has no recourse, one who cannot deflect anything

context: This poem returns to the theme of racial pride with the pearl-and-oyster simile — one of Arabic poetry's most celebrated images of inner worth concealed by outer darkness. The reassurance to Abla followed by boasting about the tribe of Abs frames the poem within Antara's dual identity as warrior-lover.

titleAr: قد أطعن الطعنة النجلاء عن عرض
titleEn: I Deliver the Wide Devastating Thrust

verses.sadr: قد أطعن الطعنة النجلاء عن عرض

verses.ajuz: لو أن ذا منك قبل اليوم معروف

verses.sadr: تنسى بلائي إذا ما غارة لقحت

translationEn: I deliver the wide devastating thrust broadside, so that the victim's brother's hand turns yellow as he bleeds out. Is it from the water-spring Suhayyah that your eyes weep? Would that this tenderness had been known from you before today. On the day she turned away and would not speak to me, she was like a gazelle at Usfan, heavy-lidded and blinking. She covered me when the stick fell — as if she were an idol regularly visited for devotion. The wealth is yours and the slave is yours — will your torment of me be turned aside today? You forget my valor when a raid blazes into fury and the tall swift she-camels emerge from it — they ride out with their saddle-girths drenched in water, urged on by young noble riders.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one who is completely drained of blood, bleeding to death
a spring or pool name; source of weeping (here a pun)
one whose eyes move rapidly (of a startled gazelle)
generous noble young men (pl. of غطريف)

context: A complex poem mixing combat description with a tender, conflicted description of a woman who showed compassion by shielding Antara when he was struck, and his ambivalent gratitude. The image of a gazelle at Usfan is a reference to a specific geography familiar to early Arab audiences.

titleAr: عمرو بن أسود فا زباء قاربة
titleEn: Amr ibn Aswad by the Water of Kullab

verses.sadr: عمرو بن أسود فا زباء قاربة
لم يسلبوها ولم يعطوا بها ثمنا

translationEn: Amr ibn Aswad — the Zubabian she-camel approaches the water of al-Kulab, collared and long-necked. They threatened me with age-darkened lances, blackened shafts from Hawman cotton — worn and tattered. They neither captured them fairly nor paid any price for them, merely grabbing like ostriches grabbing — may the cloud never water them.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a Zubabian she-camel (from the region of Zaba')
a collar or rope tied around the neck of a camel
lances with iron fittings at the base of the head
a place in Arabia known for cotton used to wrap lance shafts
like the hands of ostriches — grabbing clumsily

context: A brief satirical hija poem targeting Amr ibn Aswad, mocking his enemies' threats as hollow and their weapons as old and decrepit. The camel imagery at the opening is used to establish the scene of a watering-place where the confrontation is set.

titleEn: You Will Learn When Our Cavalry Meets
translationEn: You will know, when our cavalry meets at the bend of al-Nujayr, that your opinion was foolish. Ask Umayra, when her people assemble for battle, which tribe she will attach herself to — will she join the clan of Qays or Udhra after the banner was raised for her, and what a miserable attachment that would be! Ask Hudhayfa, when he stirred up between us a war whose banners flutter with death.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the bend/curve of al-Nujayr, a geographic feature
foolish, stupid (said of a person or their notion)
the one attached to, the ally chosen; here used with contempt
to incite, to stir up strife between parties
the hanging locks of a banner; the fluttering edges of a flag

context: A short defiant poem addressed to a specific opponent, threatening reckoning at the place called al-Nujayr. The mention of Hudhayfa as one who incited war situates the poem in a specific tribal conflict, likely part of the wars of the tribe of Abs.

titleAr: ما سابق الناس يوم الفضل مكرمة
titleEn: None Has Outrun Me to Honor

verses.sadr: ما سابق الناس يوم الفضل مكرمة

verses.ajuz: ما تعمل النار في الحلفي فتحترق
على دماه وما في جسمه رمق

verses.sadr: ولي جواد لدى الهيجاء ذو شغب
ولي حسام إذا ما سل في رهج
أنا الهزبر إذا خيل العدا طلعت
ما عبست حومة الهيجاء وجه فتى

translationEn: Whenever people have raced toward an honor on a day of virtue, I have reached it before them at the finishing line. We found Zubayd incapable of endurance when we met and the horses of death ran their race. When they turned their backs to flee, we learned on their backs what fire does to dry esparto grass as it burns. I left Khalid with birds hovering over his blood while no breath remained in his body. I was created for war — I stoke its fire when it cools and bask in its blaze where it burns me. I meet the spear-thrusts smiling in the battle-dust while horses grimace drenched in sweat. If death itself raced me while seeking to seize souls, I would reach the finish before it. I am the lion when the enemy's cavalry appears on the day of war and the brave men's blood streams — whenever battle frowns upon a warrior's face, my face turns toward it smiling.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to race one another, to compete in reaching first
esparto grass, dry desert grass that burns fiercely
no breath of life remains in him
the lion (one of the poetic names for the lion)
brave warriors, those who look sideways with pride and courage
the thick of battle, the most intense zone of fighting

context: A sustained fakhr poem built around the metaphor of racing — to glory, to battle, to death itself. Antara presents himself as outrunning not only human rivals but death itself, culminating in the famous image of smiling toward the frowning face of war.

titleEn: Perhaps He Will Grant My Uncle's Demand
translationEn: Perhaps this king will grant me what my uncle demands and bestow upon me the camels and she-herds. Does Abla know what I face in the terrors of Iraq? My uncle deceived me with pretension and treachery, tyrannizing me in his demand for the bride-price. I plunged my life into the sea of death and journeyed to Iraq without companions, driving camels and shepherds alone, returning burning more fiercely with longing. No sooner had I moved far than the dust of noble cavalry's hooves rose behind me, covering every direction as my companions lit their swords. The horsemen roared beneath the dust until I thought thunder had broken loose from its tether. I turned back knowing my uncle had deceived me with impossibilities and hypocrisy, and charged the cavalry with thrusts to their throats and collar-bones. My horse grew exhausted and fell behind — yet I drove the herds alone with my sword as if herding camels. By day's end I weakened and was taken captive, arms and legs spent. A sea of men with waves of slender spears overwhelmed me and led me to a noble king of high station. Before him I faced a grim lion, bitter to encounter, with a face like the back of a shield — and I was brought back to him in my bonds, hoping perhaps he would grant me what my uncle demands.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the bride-price, the mahr paid by the groom's side
a belt, a tether — here: 'thunder's tether' (freed thunder)
of high/noble rank, distinguished in lineage
bonds, the state of being captured and tied

context: One of the most narrative poems in the batch, telling the story of Antara's journey to Iraq to gather camels as bride-price for Abla, his ambush by cavalry, capture, and being brought before a Persian king. This episode is also elaborated in the legendary romance of Sirat Antar, suggesting this poem has roots in historical as well as legendary tradition.

titleEn: Give Them Your Final Counsel
translationEn: Give them your final counsel from those you choose among them — there is no return for you after the encounter. Between you and any embrace or reunion stands the thrusting of slender straight lances. A decisive blow from the fist of a lion of noble ancestry who surpasses all companions. Between you and Abla stands the striking of sharp swords and thrusts that make the eye-corners weep with blood. I am the hero you have heard spoken of — my fame has spread across all horizons. If the coward boasts of spending wealth, my pride is in my lean battle-trained thoroughbreds. When horsemen thrust at an enemy's chest, I thrust at their throats and collar-bones. I have surpassed all others in every excellence — is there anyone who can climb the heights as I have? Go and tell Kinda what they are about to face in a battle from which there is no escape.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a return, the possibility of going back
decisive, separating blow (the word also means 'judge')
the corners of the eyes (pl. of مأق)
horses trained and slimmed for racing or battle
the heights, the elevated positions of glory
the moonless nights; here: inescapable annihilation

context: A threatening poem likely addressed to the tribe of Kinda, combining the warrior's ultimatum with personal boasts. The poem continues the narrative of the Iraq episode, as Antara asserts that Abla can only be reached through the sword.

titleEn: How Many Lords I Left Fallen

verses.sadr: ألا يا عبل لو أبصرت فعلي

verses.ajuz: هما في الحرب كانا لي رفاقا

translationEn: How many lords have I left fallen, stirring their feet and legs in the blood. My heart sobered from its intoxication and sleep visited my eyes by stealth. Fortune favored me until my good fortune pierced the very heavens and their seven layers. I am the slave who faces death on the morning of alarm without fearing annihilation. I charge against horsemen on the day of war and do not shrink from the thin Indian blades. The swords of India thrill me until I rush yearning toward their striking edges — while others may love slim white women, I love the tawny lofty spears. The cups of spear-tips are my drink morning and evening, and the tips of Khatti lances are my morsels and my perfume when the arena grows tight. I plunged my horse's chest through the waves of death and waded the battle-dust fearing no pursuit. O Abla, if you could see my deeds when the horses of death close in around me! Ask my sword and spear about my fighting — they were my companions in war. I watered them with so much blood that if it had watered the mountains of Tihama, they would never have recovered from their drunkenness.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: morning drink and evening drink — the two main drinking times
Khatti lances, famous spears made from the wood of Khatt in eastern Arabia
the racetrack, the arena of battle
to close in, to shut around (like jaws closing)
the mountains of Tihama, the coastal plain of the Hijaz
the tawny-colored tall spears (pl. of سمراء عالية)

context: The final poem in this batch is a sweeping fakhr in which Antara inverts the conventions of love poetry — instead of longing for a beautiful woman, he declares his passion for swords and spears. The closing hyperbole about watering the mountains of Tihama with blood is one of the most extreme expressions of martial pride in classical Arabic poetry.

titleEn: Were It Not For God's Power

verses.sadr: عبل إن كان ظل القسطل الحلك
وسائلي السيف عني هل ضربت به
وسائلي الرمح عني هل طعنت به

verses.ajuz: وأتبع القرن لا أخشى من الدرك

verses.sadr: كم ضربة لي بحد السيف قاطعة

translationEn: Were it not for God whose power even kings revere, I would have made the back of my steed a dome spanning the heavens. O Abla, if the darkness of battle's dust cloud hid my fighting from your eyes, then ask my horse if I ever set it free except against a troop dark as night. Ask my sword if I ever struck with it on a day of fierce combat except at the head of a king. Ask my spear if I ever thrust it except into the armored man between throat and chin. How many a clean sword-blow and spear-thrust have I delivered, shaking the saddle-bow to its base.
context: A proud fakhr poem in which Antara invites Abla to question his weapons—his horse, sword, and spear—as witnesses to his valor. The repetitive imperative 'ask' (سائلي) creates a rhetorical crescendo celebrating his martial supremacy.

titleAr: ريح الحجاز بحق من أنشاك ردي السلام
titleEn: Wind of Hijaz, Carry My Greeting

verses.sadr: يا ريح لولا أن فيك بقية
verses.ajuz: من طيب عبلة مت قبل لقاك

verses.sadr: يا عبل ما أخشى الحمام وإنما
يا عبل لا يحزنك بعدي وابشري
هلا سألت الخيل يا ابنة مالك
verses.ajuz: إن كان بعض عداك قد أغراك

translationEn: He begs the wind of Hijaz to carry his greeting to Abla, whose fragrance alone keeps him alive. He fears not death for himself but grieves at the thought of Abla weeping. From distant Syria he reassures her of his safety, recounting how he crushed the Persians so thoroughly that even angels in the heavens heard the tumult, and then magnanimously forgave his vanquished enemies.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: created you / brought you into being

context: This poem mingles tender ghazal longing with fakhr boasting, addressing the Hijaz wind as a messenger to Abla while narrating campaigns in Syria and against the Persians. It illustrates Antara's characteristic blending of love poetry with martial glory.

titleAr: وما كنت لولا حب عبلة حائلا
titleEn: Without Abla's Love I Would Not Have Stayed

verses.sadr: وما كنت لولا حب عبلة حائلا

translationEn: Without love for Abla, the poet would not have lingered in the land where the desert shrubs grow and the beloved dwells. He hopes she may see the lightning flash over the protected pastures, and gather the fruit of the arāk trees with her own hands—a tender image of pastoral intimacy and longing.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a toothbrush tree / used for siwak

context: A brief lyrical fragment from Antara's ghazal tradition, invoking the landscape of the beloved's homeland as a metaphor for yearning. The desert flora—ghada shrubs and arāk trees—serve as markers of the beloved's territory.

titleEn: Long Have I Lingered at the Ruins

verses.ajuz: أسل الديار كفعل من لم يذهل

verses.sadr: لما سمعت دعاء مرة إذ دعا
إني امرؤ من خير عبس منصبا
إذ لا أبادر في المضيق فوارسي

verses.ajuz: لا بد أن أسقى بكأس المنهل
أني امرؤ سأموت إن لم أقتل

verses.sadr: وإذا حملت على الكريهة لم أقل

translationEn: The poem opens at the ruins of a beloved campsite where the poet stands bewildered, tears falling like scattered pearls at a dove's lament. He then shifts to a proud remembrance of battle: when the tribe of 'Abs called, he answered with spear and sword, routing the Banu 'Awf and standing foremost under the war-banner. He endures hunger willingly and never retreats, declaring that death is a watering-hole every man must drink from—and that if death were to take human form, it would look like him in desperate straits.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: open courtyards of a deserted camp

context: One of Antara's major qasidas combining the traditional nasib (amatory prelude at ruins) with powerful fakhr. The famous closing verse 'I shall die if not killed' became proverbial for the warrior's acceptance of fate and disdain for cowardice.

titleEn: Ubaylahs Wonder at the Warrior

verses.sadr: لا يكتسى إلا الحديد إذا اكتسى
قد طال ما لبس الحديد فإنما

verses.ajuz: لا خير فيك كأنها لم تحفل

verses.sadr: يا عبل كم من غمرة زهاءها
إما تريني قد نحلت ومن يكن
ذكر أشق به الجماجم في الوغى

translationEn: Ubayla mocks the poet for his unkempt warrior's appearance—gaunt, pale, wearing nothing but iron, with rust on his very skin—while he turns her ridicule into pride, explaining that it is this iron-clad body that has laid low fat, pampered heroes like her husband. The poem then pivots to an extended and exquisite description of his war-horse: its chest like smooth stone, its nostrils like the dens of a porcupine, its back like a stag's, its hooves hard as flint, its gait like a drinker in a hurry—on this steed he plunges into battle like a diving eagle.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one who appears worn and uncouth

context: This major qasida showcases Antara's mastery of the horse description (waṣf al-faras), one of the longest such passages in his diwan. The dramatic reversal—woman's scorn becoming proof of the warrior's transcendent qualities—is a hallmark of his self-presentation.

titleEn: Our Spears Suffice Our Household's Need

verses.sadr: احذر محل السوء لا تحلل به

translationEn: The ostriches stride around the empty campsite in circles, like Christians processing around a temple. The poet warns against settling in a place of evil—if a dwelling site becomes hostile, move on. Our spears alone are enough to provide for our household; let him who fails to act be shamed.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: idiom: may he be shamed / disgraced

context: A brief wisdom poem combining an evocative desert scene—ostriches circling like pilgrims—with practical tribal ethics: choose your dwelling wisely and rely on your own weapons, not others' charity.

titleEn: Tears That Stream Upon the Cheeks

verses.sadr: وها أنا ميت إن لم يعني

translationEn: Tears stream constantly down his cheeks and sleep never visits his eyes. A lover tormented by separation cannot settle or forget, no matter how long the journey stretches. Even reunion failed to quench the fire within or cool the fever of desire. He asks from time only a pure life, yet time is miserly. The final verse is his cry: he will die unless patient endurance helps him bear love's sovereignty.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a channel / stream of flowing tears

context: A moving ghazal focused entirely on the emotional devastation of separation, notable for the paradox that even reunion could not extinguish the inner fire—a mystical note on love's insatiable nature.

titleEn: They Relieved My Anguish and Healed My Ailment

verses.sadr: قسما يا عبل يا أخت المها

translationEn: The poet calls on his enemies to bring out their champions so he may grant them bitter drafts from his sword's edge, and taunts the Persians for their hesitation. Then in a sudden turn of tenderness, he swears by Abla's sweet smile and her kohl-lined eyes—the very visitant dream of her is the only sleep he knows. He closes by blessing the nights past in her company with a prayer for abundant rain.
context: This poem dramatically contrasts martial bravado—taunting the Persians in single combat—with intimate sworn devotion to Abla. The oath by her smile and her eyes is among the most tender passages in Antara's diwan.

titleEn: When the East Wind Blows at Dusk

verses.sadr: ألا يا عبل إن خانوا عهودي

translationEn: When the east wind blows at dusk, it heals his sick heart—but it brings news that his tribe has departed with his beloved, leaving him prostrated in the sand valley. He endured their broken pledges and her father's ingratitude, bearing humiliation against his will. The separation-crow has persecuted him as if he had slain one of its kin. A bird's lament on the branches mirrors his own weeping; he lends it his eyelids to cry with. He confesses that illness has become so familiar that he feels sick when well—and beneath his armor lies only a wasted silhouette whose inner blade of will still defies the sharpest sword.
context: A layered elegy of separation where nature—wind, bird, ruins—is enlisted as co-sufferer. The final image of a 'soul-blade' inside the wasted body that even a polished sword cannot dull is a metaphor for indomitable spirit beneath physical decay.

titleEn: I Fight Every Tyrant Yet Separation Kills Me

verses.sadr: غراب البين ما لك كل يوم

translationEn: He stands at crumbling ruins in the sandy valley, erased by the north wind, weeping over traces of the beloved's tribe. The separation-crow torments him daily; he addresses it half-jokingly, half-desperately, asking it to heal his heart and bring news of Ubayla. His heart wanders every land following camel tracks while his body lies as a ghost hoping for the ghost of a dream. He outweeps even the lamenting bird, and closes with the poem's immortal paradox: I fight every tyrant in battle, yet separation kills me without a single blow.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: ruins of a camp / traces of habitation

context: This poem contains one of the most celebrated lines in pre-Islamic Arabic poetry: the paradox of a warrior who defeats all enemies yet is slain by separation. It exemplifies the fusion of fakhr and ghazal that defines Antara's unique poetic voice.

titleEn: Perhaps the Days Will Bring Relief Soon
translationEn: He addresses the noblewoman's daughter with masochistic devotion: her torment is sweet and her father's injustice a form of fairness, because they belong to a clan whose glory places them above the stars. His tribe's racism—mocking his dark-skinned mother—brings him no shame; in every battle he has tales that make heroes cower. He shackles the necks of enemies, protects women with his sword, and willingly endures humiliation for those he loves. He closes with a fragile hope: perhaps after this bitter estrangement life will sweeten again.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the iron collar / chains of captivity

context: A psychologically complex poem where Antara simultaneously submits to the beloved's family hierarchy and asserts absolute martial supremacy—the paradox of a warrior-slave who is master of the battlefield yet servant in love.

titleEn: The Generous Feeders in Years of Drought

verses.sadr: فلئن صرمت الحبل يا ابنة مالك
من مثل قومي حين يختلف القنا
إنا إذا حمس الوغى نروي القنا

translationEn: A grand tribal panegyric (fakhr) beginning at eroded ruins where wind and rain have erased the beloved's traces. Antara demands that the daughter of Malik ask the kings and tribes for his record in battle: he is death incarnate when spears clash, leaving slain warriors for the wolves of the wilderness. He catalogues specific enemies routed—the tribes of Dabba, Banu Sabah—and extols his clan of 'Abs as the generous feeders when drought stretches across the land, the saviors in crisis, the protectors of women on the day of reckoning, and those who rein in the arrogant. Their swords reignite wars that have gone cold, and in times of catastrophe they are the refuge of the perishing.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the cry for help / the distressed
female ghouls / demons (simile for lean horses)

context: One of the longest fakhr poems in the batch, celebrating the 'Abs tribe's martial prowess, generosity in drought, and role as tribal protectors. The closing refrain 'the generous feeders when the years pile up in drought' became a celebrated image of Arab tribal virtue.

titleAr: لا يشرب الخمر إلا من له ذمم
titleEn: Only the Honorable May Drink Wine

verses.sadr: لا يشرب الخمر إلا من له ذمم
verses.ajuz: ولا يبيت له جار على وجل

verses.sadr: ولا تقتض الدين إلا بالقنا الذبل
verses.ajuz: ولا تحكم سوى الأسياف في القلل

verses.sadr: ولا تفر إذا ما خضت معركة
verses.ajuz: فما يزيد فرار المرء في الأجل

verses.sadr: يا عبل أنت سواد القلب فاحتكمي
verses.ajuz: في مهجتي واعدلي يا غاية الأمل

verses.sadr: وإن ترحلت من عبس فلا تقفي
verses.ajuz: في دار ذل ولا تصغي إلى العذل
تبقى بلا فارس يدعى ولا بطل

verses.sadr: سلي فزارة عن فعلي وقد نفرت
تهز سمر القنا حقدا علي وقد
يخبرك بدر بن عمرو أنني بطل
verses.ajuz: ألقى الجيوش بقلب قد من جبل
والطعن في إثرهم أمضى من الأجل
وما أبكي لفرقة أصحاب ولا طلل

verses.sadr: بل من فراق التي في جفنها سقم
verses.ajuz: قد زادني عللا منه على عللي

verses.sadr: أمسي على وجل خوف الفراق كما
verses.ajuz: تمسي الأعادي من سيفي على وجل

verses.sadr: من لي برد الصبا واللهو والغزل
verses.ajuz: هيهات ما فات من أيامك الأول

verses.sadr: طوى الجديدان ما قد كنت أنشره
وما ثنى الدهر عزمي عن مهاجمة
في الخيل والخافقات السود لي شغل

verses.ajuz: فلست أبكي على رسم ولا طلل
هل فاتني بطل أو حلت عن بطل

verses.sadr: لا يشرب الخمر إلا من له ذمم
verses.ajuz: ولا يبيت له جار على وجل

translationEn: An aristocratic code of honor opens and closes this poem: only the man of pledges may drink wine, only the man whose neighbor sleeps safely at night. Debts are collected only with spears, judgments rendered only by swords. He counsels Abla to leave any abode of humiliation and ignore blamers, for without him the tribe's land would be empty of heroes. He challenges the tribe of Fazara, whose cavalry Badr ibn 'Amr can attest he shattered, returning from battle so joyful he was like a drunken celebrant. Yet separation frightens him as much as his sword frightens his enemies—and the poet laments the lost days of youth and love, now folded away by time.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the dark of the heart (deepest part / core)
the din and heat of battle

context: A poem of moral instruction framed around the warrior's code, containing the celebrated opening aphorism that wine belongs only to the man of honor. It moves through martial boasting to tender regret for lost youth and love.

titleEn: Life with Humiliation Is Hell

verses.ajuz: واقدم إذا حق اللقا في الأول
أو مت كريما تحت ظل القسطل

verses.sadr: موت الفتى في عزه خير له
verses.ajuz: من أن يبيت أسير طرف أكحل

verses.sadr: إن كنت في عدد العبيد فهمتي
قد طال عزكم وذلي في الهوى

translationEn: One of Antara's most aphoristic poems, opening with wisdom maxims of warrior ethics: face oppression with equal force, ignore cowards' counsel, choose an elevated station or die nobly in battle—for no fortress saves you from fate. He declares that though counted among slaves, his ambition soars above the Pleiades, and that his spear and sword—not kinship or numbers—earned him his glory. He enumerates enemies slain from Banu Huraiqa to Rabi'a and Zubarqan, then in a stunning reversal describes his dark-skinned mother with affection: legs like an ostrich's, hair like peppercorns, and a smile beneath the veil like lightning in deep darkness. He closes refusing the water of life drunk in humiliation, preferring the bitter cup of colocynth drunk in honor.
context: This poem contains Antara's most famous statement of identity pride: his aspiration soars above the stars even if he is counted among slaves. The tender description of his African mother, ending with her smile like lightning, is remarkable for its time in humanizing an enslaved woman.

titleEn: A Heart the Blamer Cannot Sway
translationEn: His heart cannot be swayed by reproach and his eyes barely sleep—misfortune has hardened him until fate's beauty and ugliness feel alike. He turns to address a rival named 'Amr who has threatened him with empty words: we shall see which of us is left pierced on the ground, whose wife is taken captive and weeps in bereavement. You dare remember Abla while she is guarded by a fearsome lion (Antara himself), and you seek to face me whose sword the very mountain could not withstand.
context: A tight, aggressive poem of challenge directed at a named enemy 'Amr, combining the lover's sleepless longing with the warrior's direct confrontation. The image of a lion guarding Abla's tent identifies Antara himself as the guardian beast.

titleEn: Come Fight Me, O Misfortunes of Nights

verses.sadr: إن لي همة أشد من الصخر
وجوادا ما سار إلا سرى البرق
يا سباع الفلا إذا اشتعل الحرب
ثم عودي من بعد ذا واشكريني

translationEn: Antara defies the misfortunes of the night to attack him from right and left—he barely even notices them. His resolve is harder than rock, stronger than fixed mountains. His spear-point guides him through darkness, and his black horse with a crescent blaze between its eyes runs like lightning whose hooves spark fire; he and the horse ransom each other with their lives. When the marketplace of battle opens and burnished blades blaze, he is its broker and his lance the merchant buying precious souls. He then turns to invite the beasts of the wilderness to follow him to the feast of enemies' skulls, which he offers as food for their cubs.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the white blaze on a horse's forehead

context: A defiant and richly imagined fakhr poem notable for its invitation to the beasts of prey to follow Antara into battle as scavengers—a dark inversion of pastoral imagery that underscores his absolute self-confidence in battle.

titleAr: سلي يا عبل عمرا عن فعالي
titleEn: Ask Amr About My Deeds, O Abla

verses.sadr: سلي يا عبل عمرا عن فعالي

verses.ajuz: إذا ما قال ظنك في مقالي

verses.sadr: إذا ما سل سال دما نجيعا

translationEn: He asks Abla to consult 'Amr about how he answered the enemies who attacked in darkness on lean-flanked horses, each a tyrant twisting his mustache. When they kindled the fire of death with their spear-tips, a black lion of 'Abs quenched it with a polished bright sword that, when drawn, poured dark blood and would pierce even solid mountains. His spear-tip glows like a crescent moon, and when he wields it in his right hand, death races to keep up on his left. He kept his promise to Abla, for had he broken it, the base men would have said he grew cold toward her—an unacceptable dishonor.
context: A compact fakhr poem structured as a report to Abla about a night raid, framed by the motive of keeping his pledge to her. The closing verse ties martial honor directly to love's fidelity—breaking his promise would prove him faithless to her.

titleEn: He Desires the One Who Torments Him

verses.sadr: لو كان قلبي معي ما اخترت غيركم
verses.ajuz: ولا رضيت سواكم في الهوى بدلا
فليس يقبل لا لوما ولا عذلا

translationEn: If his heart were his own, he would choose no one but the beloved and accept no substitute in love. But his heart is a willful runaway that desires precisely the one who torments it, and so refuses to accept any blame or rebuke—for the heart is beyond reason when love commands it.
context: A brief philosophical ghazal couplet on the paradox of a heart that chooses its own tormentor—a theme that anticipates later Sufi poetry about the soul that loves what destroys it.

titleAr: دع ما مضى لك في الزمان الأول
titleEn: Leave What Has Passed in Former Times

verses.sadr: دع ما مضى لك في الزمان الأول
إن كنت أنت قطعت برا مقفرا

verses.ajuz: لا مؤنس لي غير حد المنصل

translationEn: A poem of self-comparison and boast: he tells a rival to leave past glories aside and act in the present. If the rival crossed empty deserts at night with an army, Antara traveled alone under the Pleiades, companioned only by his sword-edge. He describes a supernatural night journey: the full moon racing overhead like a hurrying rider, the vulture hurtling toward the Arcturus star, a ghoul flickering before him like a torch with red eyes and scythe-like claws, while jinn howl in the forests—and all of them scatter screaming at the sight of his sword, like camels panicking around a camp. These are nights whose mere retelling would turn a newborn's hair white.
context: A vivid supernatural night journey narrative that reads like a folkloric adventure, situating Antara's heroism in a cosmos of stars, ghouls, and jinn—all of whom flee his sword. This nightscape poetry is rare in pre-Islamic verse and reflects the genre's imaginal possibilities.

titleEn: The Punishment of Estrangement Brought Union
translationEn: The patient endurance of estrangement ultimately brings reunion, and true patience reveals what seemed impossible. He freed noble women and maidens, leaving no one in bondage—driven only by love for Abla. He blames fate for humbling a man of his resolve. The body of the poem records a battle against the combined tribes of Tayy and Kalb who arrived with an earth-filling army on lean war-horses; they fled in confusion, while only a slave—Antara himself—turned back the reins of war when the noblemen of lineage faltered. The enemy cavalry fled lighter than they came, their fallen chiefs used as horseshoes by their own panicked mounts.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the impossible / what was deemed unattainable
the women's riding litters / the women's camp

context: A remarkable poem where Antara explicitly names himself 'a slave' who alone turned back the enemy when noblemen of pedigree failed—a pointed assertion that courage, not birth, determines nobility. The image of the enemy's skulls becoming horseshoes for their own fleeing cavalry is among his most grotesque battle images.

titleAr: يا صاحبي لا تبك ربعا قد خلا
titleEn: Friend, Do Not Weep at the Empty Campsite

verses.sadr: يا صاحبي لا تبك ربعا قد خلا
من أين تدري الدار أنك عاشق

verses.ajuz: لو لم يذق مني المرارة ماحلا
دارت بها في الغاب غربان الفلا
إن كنتما عن أرض عبس تعدلا
خط المشيب على شبابي ما علا

verses.sadr: بل لو صدمت بهمتي جبلي حرى
لو لم تكن يا قيس غرك جاهل
verses.ajuz: ما سقت نحو ديار عنتر جحفلا

verses.sadr: يا قيس أنت تعد نفسك سيدا

verses.ajuz: إن كنت ممن عقله قد أكملا

translationEn: He urges his companion to stop weeping at empty ruins—the house does not know your love, and the only true messenger when a friend betrays is the spear-point. He has hardened fate itself to bitterness. He then commissions his two companions as messengers to 'Abs: tell Qays and al-Rabi' that grey hair cannot dim my resolve—if I struck the mountains Hira and Abu Qubays with my will, they would shake. He warns Qays that it was a fool's counsel that led him against Antara's territory, and advises him to follow his noble father's example instead of seeking blood feuds with Fazara. The closing line: I left nothing in their homeland except keening women wailing in the open desert.
context: A poem of combined wisdom and warning, notable for its dramatic embassy structure: two companions carry his message to named tribal enemies. The closing image of empty land filled only with wailing women is his signature battle's-aftermath motif.

titleEn: Have the Poets Left Any Theme Untouched

verses.ajuz: أم هل عرفت الدار بعد توهم

verses.sadr: ولقد شربت من المدامة بعد ما
وإذا صحوت فما أقصر عن ندى
هلا سألت الخيل يا ابنة مالك
verses.ajuz: إن كنت جاهلة بما لم تعلمي

verses.sadr: إذ لا أزال على رحالة سابح

verses.ajuz: مني وبيض الهند تقطر من دمي

verses.sadr: يا شاة ما قنص لمن حلت له

verses.ajuz: إذ تقلص الشفتان عن وضح الفم

verses.sadr: في حومة الحرب التي لا تشتكي
إذ يتقون بي الأسنة لم أخم

verses.ajuz: ما قد علمت وبعض ما لم تعلمي
وزوت جواني الحرب من لم يجرم

verses.sadr: ولقد خشيت بأن أموت ولم تدر

translationEn: This is the opening of Antara's immortal Mu'allaqa—one of the seven great odes of pre-Islamic Arabia. It begins with the famous rhetorical question 'Have the poets left anything to say?' at the deserted campsite of the beloved. He describes her with intoxicating similes: eyes of a solitary gazelle fawn, breath like a perfume merchant's sachet, or a virgin meadow watered by untainted rains. She sleeps on soft cushions while he sleeps on the saddle-back of his black bridled horse. A long camel description follows her. He boasts that he is gentle when treated justly and terrible when wronged. He describes killing a warrior whose armor he tears open, leaving him for the vultures—and the moment of inspiration in battle when the cavalry cry 'Advance, Antara!' heals his soul. The poem ends with the enmity of the sons of Dumdum who vowed his blood, but whom he answers: their father I already left as carrion for eagles.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the theme that has been exhausted / trodden over
the back teeth / grinning with bared teeth

context: This is the Mu'allaqa of Antara ibn Shaddad, the most celebrated poem in his diwan and one of the seven canonical Mu'allaqat of pre-Islamic Arabia. Its opening question and its description of the beloved have been quoted and analyzed more than any other passage in his work. The poem integrates nasib, fakhr, battle narrative, and self-defense in a single unified masterpiece.

titleEn: With Every Keen Blade That Pierces Through
translationEn: He praises his warrior companions armed with keen blades that pierce between the ribs like the collar of a leather jerkin. His horsemen march with coats of fine Syrian mail upon them, glowing like burning coals. Among them are free, bright-faced men of trust, unlike those dark-faced treacherous tribes he has seen. When their camels broke into a run toward the enemy's watering pools, they chose between killing and capturing spoils. And if an ally betrays, they are leopards who mark noses—a vivid image of unmistakable vengeance.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: penetration / the ability to pierce through
we mark their noses / we take vengeance visibly

context: A tightly crafted fakhr fragment celebrating Antara's warrior band and their code of combat and loyalty. The simile of men blazing like burning coals in their mail is one of his characteristic light-and-fire images in battle description.

titleEn: Ruqash Comes Only for Brief Visits

verses.sadr: عجوز من بني حام بن نوح:

translationEn: A poem that begins with a resigned lover's lament—Ruqash visits only rarely and the bond with her is like a worn-out rope. He stands with companions at a watering stop, spotting women's litters moving in the dusk. Then the poem pivots to a thrilling battle scene: his trotting war-horses scatter dust and death, facing an army of blood-thirsty warriors armed with Indian blades like flames. They come like a cold cloud-front; Antara's force arrives as wildfire in a thicket. He silences all sounds but the din of combat, scattering their ranks on his battle-scarred horse whose saddle-straps are like fine cloth ribbons. The poem closes with self-identification: led by a young man of the finest 'Abs stock, whose mother is of the line of Ham son of Noah—the poet's proud claim of his African maternal heritage, with his mother's forehead compared to the sacred black stone.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: women's litters / travelling caravan of women
a thin fine curtain / decorated cloth
the sacred stone of the Standing Place (at Mecca)

context: This poem's remarkable closing image—comparing his mother's forehead to the sacred Maqam stone at Mecca—is both a boast of her dignity and a spiritual elevation of his African lineage. The poem moves from melancholic ghazal to furious battle narrative to proud tribal self-definition.

titleEn: Abla Lingers in Her Veil While I Fight

verses.sadr: لما سمعت نداء مرة قد علا

translationEn: Abla rests in her veiled seclusion trailing her robes while Antara is encased in the links of dark iron. He sees spoils he could seize but his sense of modesty and propriety holds him back. He wishes she could see him advancing in battle like a lion. The enemies are small as locusts and large as frogs in a flooded pond. When the cry of Murra and the sons of Rabi'a rose in the dark dust, and Muhallem led his men under a banner of death, he knew there would be a blow that scatters nestlings from their perches. They call his name while swords flash like lightning in a dark cloud and armor gleams like frogs' eyes in a dark pool—and their wives bring medicinal herbs to heal the fallen.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the iron chain-links / coat of mail

context: A poem rich in zoological similes for the enemy—frogs, locusts, nestlings—contrasted with Antara as a lion. The detail that his restraint (tashum) prevents him from taking spoils is a rare moment of code-ethics in his battle poetry, underlining his self-control even at victory.

titleEn: I Wept at Bitter Separation Though Steadfast

verses.ajuz: صبور على طعن القنا لو علمتم

verses.sadr: وأطمع من دهري بما لا أناله
verses.ajuz: وألزم منه ذل من ليس يرحم

verses.sadr: وأرجو التداني منك يا ابنة مالك

verses.ajuz: إذا عاد عني كيف بات المتيم

verses.sadr: ولا تجزعي إن لج قومك في دمي
verses.ajuz: فما لي بعد الهجر لحم ولا دم

verses.sadr: ألم تسمعي نوح الحمائم في الدجى
ولم يبق لي يا عبل شخص معرف
وإن عشت من بعد الفراق فما أنا
وإن نام جفني كان نومي علالة

translationEn: He wept at the agony of separation yet is steadfast against lance-thrusts—a paradox he offers to those who do not know him. He will conceal his longing in his heart, stay awake while blamers sleep, and hope for approach to Abla though fire-wars stand between. He begs her to send her phantom image in a dream and ask it how the infatuated one fared. He tells her not to grieve if her people seek his blood—there is no flesh or blood left in him after estrangement. Doves in the darkness learned their mourning from his grief. All that remains of him is a burning liver dissolving into illness, decaying bones, and a ribcage besieged by the army of rejection. If he survives separation, his love-claim will ring hollow—and his sleep is nothing but a hoping for the visitant dream.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the burning / scorched liver (center of emotion)

context: A deeply introspective ghazal focusing on the progressive physical dissolution of the lover's body under separation's siege. The image of the dove learning its mourning from the poet's grief is a reversal of the usual nasib convention where the bird provokes human tears.

titleEn: That Is Abla's Fire, O My Companion
translationEn: He spots Abla's campfire in the pitch-dark night and tells his drinking companion—that fire has lit up the darkness, and its twin burns in his own heart as a fire of longing that only grows when fanned. A white-skinned maiden swaying like a branch in the breeze kindled it; her saliva is sweeter than honey mixed with wine, yet every cool sip from her lips burns like hellfire in his mouth. The full moon stole its beauty from her, and gazelles of the wilderness borrowed their magic from her eyelids. His passion is everlasting torment. He then pivots to praise a patron king: kings prostrate themselves at his mere mention, and when he marches, death races ahead of him to reach his enemies before he arrives.
context: A poem that opens with a campfire becoming a symbol of dual fire—the beloved's hearth and the lover's heart. The paradox of the beloved's cool lips tasting like hellfire is a sophisticated oxymoron. The closing panegyric to a powerful unnamed king suggests court performance context.

titleEn: One Day in Honor's Shade Surpasses a Thousand
translationEn: Zubaiba reproaches him for his recklessness in the crush of battle, fearing he will meet death by spear or sword. He rejects her counsel as unworthy of noble men—only the base accept such logic. A gray-bearded elder plunges into the sea of death and returns unharmed, while death comes for an infant in its cradle before weaning. His maxim: do not accept diminishment and humiliation, settling for scraps. One day lived in honor's shadow is worth more than a thousand years in the shade of abasement.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the crumbled / broken remnants of worldly goods

context: One of Antara's most frequently quoted wisdom poems, built around the immortal closing aphorism contrasting one day of honor against a millennium of humiliation—a verse that became proverbial in Arabic literature and is still widely cited.

titleAr: أحب بني عبس ولو هدروا دمي
titleEn: I Love the Banu Abs Even If They Shed My Blood

verses.sadr: أحب بني عبس ولو هدروا دمي
سلي يا ابنة العبسي رمحي وصارمي
verses.ajuz: وما فعلا في يوم حرب الأعاجم

verses.sadr: وكم فارس يا عبل غادرت ثاويا

translationEn: He opens with the most paradoxical declaration of tribal loyalty: he loves the Banu 'Abs even if they declare his blood lawful and call him an oppressor. He then asks Abla—daughter of 'Abs—to question his spear and sword about what they did in the Persian wars. He fed them the blood of enemies mixed with bitter gall. On a pure-blooded Arab mare that flies when battle intensifies and whinnies in fear while slithering from spear-tips like a black snake, he plunged into the sea of death. Many a warrior he left behind biting his hands in remorse, while desert beasts roll his body and flocks of great vultures dive on him from the sky.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: declared my blood permissible / halal to shed
black snakes (simile for the slithering mare)

context: The opening paradox—loving a tribe that outlaws your blood—is one of Antara's most psychologically rich statements, reflecting his ambiguous position as the illegitimate son whose valor constantly exceeds but can never fully resolve his social outsider status.

titleEn: A Heart That Wine Cannot Console

verses.sadr: ألا يا عبل قد شمت الأعادي

verses.ajuz: تشيب من له في المهد عام

translationEn: A heart that even wine cannot console, a body perpetually sick, eyelids that bleed in the night's darkness—this is the lover's state. A cooing dove wounds his heart with a sweet voice; he turns entirely to the memory of Abla, telling his companion 'this is my only aspiration.' In the Hijaz, her people have made union with her forbidden. She dwells among her tribe's tents—a full-figured, unveiled beauty whose healthy eyes conceal sickness, and whose lips hold musk and camphor mingled with wine. The full moon borrows its completeness from her face; the willow branch borrows its grace from her sway. Though enemies gloat at his distance, his journey has brought him suffering enough to whiten a newborn's hair—and then ease, and an indescribable king whose generosity flows from his palms like sea or rain-cloud. The sun has crowned this ruler; all the world's kings pray toward him; he is humanity's soul. The poem closes with a prayer: 'Advance, O master of both worlds, and endure as long as the dove laments.'

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the crying bird / one who calls out
the stars of the Great Bear (Ursa Major)
mankind and jinn / the two weighty creations

context: The final poem of this batch blends personal ghazal anguish with a cosmic panegyric to a great ruler whose throne rests on the stars of Ursa Major and whose canopy is the sky itself. The prayer closing—'endure as long as the dove laments'—ties the love motif of mourning doves to the poem's political benediction.

titleEn: Nothing Startled Me but Separation and Its Cruelty

verses.sadr: فكأن من قد غاب جاء مواصلي

translationEn: Nothing startled me but separation and its injustice, so I obeyed it while fate hangs from my reins. Leave the blamers to prolong their reproach — I am a friend to blame and blamers alike. The beloved draws near even when his dwelling is far, through a phantom that visited in dreams. It is as if one who was absent came to visit me, and I wave to him in greeting. I have endured hardships and calamities until I ascended to the most honored station. I overpowered the champions of battle until they lay wounded and slain by the strokes of my sword. Longing has stirred — pour me a cup of aged wine until the sun sets beneath the dark.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to prolong, to go on at length
phantom, apparition (of a beloved in dreams)

context: A poem blending ghazal tenderness with martial fakhr. Antara opens with the shock of separation but immediately channels it through his warrior resolve — enduring blame, dreaming of the absent beloved, and recounting battlefield victories. The final image of wine poured until sunset reflects the pre-Islamic convention of drowning sorrow in drink, used here ironically against his own sorrow.

titleEn: He Answers the Conscience's Subtle Signals

verses.sadr: وإني عزيز الجار في كل موطن
وقد خيروني كأس خمر فلم أجد
verses.ajuz: سوى لوعة في الحرب ذات ضرام
عوابسا عليها كرام في سروج كرام

verses.sadr: ولا تذكرا لي طيب عيش فإنما
وفي الغزو ألقى أرغد العيش لذة
verses.ajuz: وفي المجد لا في مشرب وطعام
جرى لأبعد شأو من بعيد مرام

translationEn: He answers the conscience's subtle signals and spares you the need for whip or bridle. I have the might of one with muscular arms, drowsy like a lion defending his cubs. I honor my neighbor in every land and ennoble myself against lowering my station. I abandoned towering houses for the gleam of bright swords beneath the canopy of dust. They offered me a cup of wine but I found nothing but burning anguish in the fire of battle. I shall depart from you and not visit your dwellings, seeking them instead in every cloak of darkness. I pursue enemies with every swift and noble warrior and every lion-bold in combat. I forbade sleep until I led them — stern-faced riders on honored horses in honored saddles. They brandish spears in their hands as if they had drunk pure wine from the breast. When leveled for the thrust they looked like stars guided by full moons. And white swords in the shadow of the dust-cloud, like drops of lutes in the darkness of clouds. Sing to me the neigh of horses — that is my music, and the shimmer of blood is my cup-companion. Rest my saddle on the burning sand — that is my resting place, and the fluttering of banners my tent. Do not remind me of a pleasant life, for attaining hopes is my health and my sickness alike. In raiding I find the richest pleasure of life and in glory, not in drink and food. Why should I accept humiliation when my sharp sword is bold at necks and never blunt? I have a horse that imitates the winds when it runs to the most distant goal and furthest aim. Is it injustice — while my spear stands by me and my sword — and abasement — while my glory holds my reins?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: drowsy, lying in wait (said of a lion)

context: A long fakhr poem showcasing Antara's full warrior identity. The opening image of a horse so attuned to its rider it needs no whip is one of the great horse descriptions in Arabic poetry. The poem then enumerates all martial virtues — guarding neighbors, scorning comfort, wielding sword and spear — and climaxes in the paradox that his glory itself holds his reins.

titleEn: The Women Cry Out in Fear of Captivity

verses.sadr: يا جفوني إن لم تجودي بدمع
لا رفعت الحسام في الحرب حتى

translationEn: The full moon was eclipsed at its completion and its light was hidden, turning back to darkness. The bright planets set and disappeared, and the luminance of the horizons became thick dust — when they said that Zuhayr had fallen slain, grief pitched its tent among us and settled. Time gave him the cup of death to drink, and thus does time water the doves. He was my support and my equipment in calamities — he was my armor, my long spear, and my sword. O my eyelids, if you will not be generous with tears I shall forbid sleep to you forever. I swear by Him who gives death and life and who governs souls and bodies: I shall not raise the sword in battle until I leave the people as bones in the wilderness. O sons of Amir, you shall meet a lightning-bolt from my sword that streams blood in torrents. And the women cry out in fear of captivity and weep over the young orphans.
context: An elegy and revenge oath for a fallen comrade named Zuhayr. The cosmic imagery of the full moon eclipsing and stars disappearing is a classical ritha convention signaling the magnitude of the loss. Antara swears a solemn oath before God to avenge the death, threatening Banu Amir with devastation — the women crying and children orphaned.

titleEn: Halt, My Two Friends at Dawn, and Greet

verses.ajuz: وعوجا فإن لم تفعلا اليوم تندما

verses.sadr: على طلل لو أنه كان قبله
أيا عزنا لا عز في الناس مثله
verses.ajuz: على عهد ذي القرنين لن يتهدما
علوت بها بيتا من المجد معلما

verses.sadr: إذا ما ابتدرنا النهب من بعد غارة
ألا رب يوم قد أنخنا بدراهم

verses.ajuz: من الناس إلا راحت بالدم ملئت دما

translationEn: Halt, my two friends, at dawn and give peace, and turn aside — if you do not do so today you will regret it. Halt at a ruin, whose effaced traces would have spoken if only they could speak before now. O our glory, no glory among people matches it — from the age of Dhul-Qarnayn it shall never crumble. When the tribe of Abs marches behind me with spears, I raise for them a banner of glory most high. You see them mustering swift horses and long spears, high-necked, mounted on roan and jet-black steeds. When we race to the spoils after a raid we stir up dust as black as pitch with our hooves. Many a day we have camped among enemies I straightened my sword and my balanced spear against. No people ever raised a banner to meet us in battle without their banner returning soaked in blood. We annihilated their gathering with our spears and we struck down their champion and shattered him — with every fine-edged Indian sword, a blade that is resolute when it meets the blow. Its point splits the skulls of the armored men and slices from the champions hand and forearm.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: tip of a sword or spear

context: A classic qasida structure: opening with the ruins of the beloved's dwelling, pivoting to tribal fakhr. The address to two companions at ruins (atlal) is the canonical pre-Islamic opening. The martial boasts that follow glorify Banu Abs's raids and Antara's own swordsmanship, concluding with a vivid description of the lethal blade.

titleAr: عليك أيا عبيلة كل يوم سلام
titleEn: Upon You, O Ubaylah, Each Day a Peace
translationEn: Upon you, O Ubaylah, each day a peace — peace upon peace upon peace. The phantom of Abla came to me in sleep and kissed me three times through the veil. Then it bade me farewell and left in me a burning I conceal, yet it blazes in my bones. If only I could be alone with myself and quench with tears the fire of my longing. O daughter of Malik, how can I find solace when my love for you goes back to weaning? How can I ever seek closeness to you when around your tent stand lions of the thicket? By the right of your love, I shall treat my heart with nothing but patience, O daughter of the noble. Until I climb the steps of glory by the thrust of the spear or the stroke of the sword. I am the slave whose story you have heard — I tended the camels of my people from the time of my weaning. I go from dawn to nightfall and I sleep among the tent-ropes. I submit myself to Abla from the excess of my longing and make her my sole concern in the world. I obey the commands of her father while love has seized my reins entirely. I have accepted her love willingly and against my will — shall I win her before death? If she reproaches my blackness it is my pride, for I am a knight descended from Ham. I have a heart harder than mountains and my fame spreads like the fragrance of musk. The wonder of it is: I hunt lions by force and prey on wild beasts like insects — yet the gazelles of al-Sa'd set a snare for me, and the doe-eyes of al-Sharba and the lavender conquer me. By your father's life, I shall never forget her love even if love for her grinds my very bones. Upon you, O Ubaylah, each day a peace — peace upon peace upon peace.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the veil worn over the mouth
to prey on, to devour as a predator

context: One of Antara's tenderest ghazal poems, built around a triple-repeated refrain of 'peace upon peace.' The dream-vision of Abla kissing him is contrasted with the waking reality of being forbidden from her. The poem contains Antara's signature self-deprecation about his slave status and dark skin, transformed into pride through warrior identity.

titleAr: أنا في الحرب العوان غير مجهول المكان
titleEn: In the Raging War I Am Not Unknown

verses.sadr: أنا في الحرب العوان غير مجهول المكان
verses.ajuz: أينما نادى المنادي في دجى النقع يراني

translationEn: In the raging war I am not unknown — wherever the caller calls in the darkness of battle-dust he sees me. My sword and my spear are the two witnesses to my deeds — that I thrust my adversary while he is brave of heart. I give him the cup of death to drink and the drink is near from him. I kindle the fire with my valor and quench it with my courage. I am a scowling lion with no equal in creation. The spear was made for my hand and the Indian sword — they were with me in the cradle, resting on my chest as two companions. When the earth turns red like rose-oil and the blood flows over it in a crimson scarlet — and I see the horses falling headlong through the wide plain — then pour me a drink, not from a cup but from blood red as crimson dye. Let me hear the melody of swords until they delight me. The sweetest of sounds to me is the ring of the Indian blade and the creak of the spear out loud in battle on the day of the thrust — and the war cry of men as it descends upon the champions.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: raging, fierce (war that has been fought before)
battle-dust, clouds of dust in combat

context: A bravura fakhr poem in short rajaz-like verses, building to the famous declaration that Antara's music is the ring of steel and the war cry. The image of being rocked to sleep in the cradle with sword and spear — his two companions from birth — is one of his most celebrated self-portraits.

titleAr: أحبك يا ظلوم فأنت عندي مكان الروح
titleEn: I Love You, O Unjust One, You Are to Me Like the Soul
translationEn: I love you, O unjust one — you are to me like the soul within the body of a coward. But if I were to say you are in the place of my soul, I would fear for you the sudden thrust of the spear.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one who is unjust, the beloved who wrongs the lover
the place of the soul, as essential as the soul
the sudden first thrust of the spear, the opening attack

context: A two-verse gem of the ghazal tradition. Antara uses a warrior's logic to express love: placing the beloved in the position of his soul would endanger her in battle. The paradox — that loving her too openly endangers her — is characteristic of Antara's fusion of martial and romantic registers.

titleEn: O King Whose Palms Stand in Place of Rain

verses.sadr: يا قبلة القصاد يا تاج العلا
verses.ajuz: يا بدر هذا العصر في كيوانه

verses.sadr: ما ليس يوصف أو يقدر أو

verses.ajuz: من كل فن لاح في أفنانه

verses.sadr: ملك إذا ما جال في يوم اللقا

translationEn: O king whose open palms stand in place of rain in his times — O direction of petitioners' faces, O crown of glory, O full moon of this age in its station. O shamer of the rain-star with his generosity, O rescuer of the grieved from his sorrows. O dwellers of Abs's abodes, I have received from Kisra and from his beneficence what cannot be described or measured or do justice to by any tongue. A king who has gathered all the ranks of glory with an elevated honor that dwells in his palace. A lord through whom the age and its people are ennobled, and time itself has gained pride from his crowns. When he strikes, all creatures fear his might — and even the lion fears him at the sight of him. He who makes justice manifest in his days through his qualities, and equity in his lands. I spent the evening in a fertile quarter of his, strolling in it and in his garden. I saw his blessing overflow and its water telling of his gifts and the generosity of his fingers. In a spring encampment where spring gathered in its quarter every art that shone in its branches. And birds of every kind sang openly that all of time is in submission to his rein. A king who, when he roams on the day of encounter, leaves the enemy standing in bewilderment at his affair. Victory is among his companions before all others, and fortune and favor are among his supporters. I shall therefore give thanks for his deed publicly and shall joust with the horsemen in his arena.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: those who set out to seek a patron, petitioners
Saturn (used to denote the highest station)
the rain-star, the luck of the sky bringing rain
royal hall, vaulted palace hall (Sasanid term)
branches (of trees), also varieties and types
his sitting companions, his court intimates

context: A court panegyric (madh) addressed to a king described in terms recalling the Sasanid Kisra (Khusraw). This is unusual for Antara, who rarely composed formal panegyric, preferring self-praise. The lush garden scene and catalog of royal virtues — generosity, justice, martial power — follow the classical madh structure.

titleEn: When My Adversary Demands His Debt
translationEn: When my adversary demands a debt from me I repay the debt with the Rudaynian spear. The edge of the sword satisfies us all and judges between you and me with justice. You have misjudged my worth, O sons of the despicable — but the people of the two horizons know it well. The hand of calamities has not broken my pillar, nor has the finger of my doom reached me. I have risen with my sharp sword and the tip of my spear above the horizon of Suha and the two Farkadayn stars. I left the dueling ground in the middle of a desert as he scraped his cheek and temples in the dust. How many a horseman at my sword's touch has lain with a shattered skull and blood-dyed hands — while the eagles of doom wheel above him and the ravens of separation hop around him. And another flees in terror of my presence, shedding tears from both eyes. I shall annihilate your gathering with my patience, and my burning anguish will be quenched and my eye satisfied.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: made by Rudayna, a woman renowned for crafting spears (attributive)
the two horizons, east and west
vicissitudes of fate, calamities of time
al-Suha, a dim star (used to denote the highest height)
the two Farkadayn stars near the North Star
my burning anguish, the fire of my grief

context: A powerful fakhr poem structured around the metaphor of 'debts repaid with spears.' The catalog of defeated enemies — skulls shattered, blood on hands, ravens circling — contrasts with the enemy's cowardly flight. The final verse promises the speaker's anguish will be quenched only through complete victory.

titleAr: يا طائر البان قد هيجت أشجاني
titleEn: O Bird of the Ban Tree, You Have Stirred My Sorrows

verses.sadr: يا طائر البان قد هيجت أشجاني
إن كنت تندب إلفا قد فجعت به
زدني من النوح واسعدني على حزني
verses.ajuz: حتى ترى عجبا من فيض أجفاني

verses.sadr: وقف لتنظر ما بي لا تكن عجلا
وطر لعلك في أرض الحجاز ترى
verses.ajuz: ركبا على عالج أو دون نعمان

verses.sadr: ناشدتك الله يا طير الحمام إذا

translationEn: O bird of the ban tree, you have stirred my sorrows and increased my longing — O bird of the ban tree. If you are mourning a companion you were bereft of, then the same thing that grieved you through separation has grieved me. Add more of your lamentation and help me in my grief until you see a wonder from the overflow of my eyelids. Pause to see what ails me, do not be hasty — and beware yourself of the breaths of my inner fire. Then fly, perhaps in the land of Hejaz you will see a caravan at Aalij or near Nuaman, traveling with a young woman whose tears pour down in longing for a far homeland and neighbors. I adjure you by God, O dove, when you one day see the laden camels of the tribe, cry for me — and tell them: 'We left one prostrate whose tears have run dry as he weeps in scarlet blood.'

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: bird of the ban (moringa) tree, a symbol of separation
stirred my sorrows, made me grieve
my inner fire, the blaze of my passion
lament for me, announce my death (naah)

context: A ghazal in the classical mould of addressing a bird as messenger — here the ban-tree bird and the dove. The poet asks the bird to fly to Hejaz and find his beloved's caravan, then report back that he lies prostrate weeping tears of blood. The symmetry of the bird's grief and the poet's grief is a hallmark of the genre.

titleEn: For Whom Are These Ruins at al-Raqmatain That Grieve Me

verses.ajuz: غراب به ما بي من الهيمان

verses.sadr: ينوح على إلف له وإذا شكا
ألا يا غراب البين لو كنت صاحبي
عسى أن نرى من نحو عبلة مخبرا
وقد هتفت في جنح ليل حمامة
فقلت لها لو كنت مثلي حزينة
وما كنت ذي دوح تميس غصونه
أيا عبل لو أن الخيال يزورني
لئن غبت عن عيني يا ابنة مالك
دعوا الموت يأتيني على أي صورة أتى

translationEn: Whose are these ruins at al-Raqmatain that grieve me — the hands of decay have wrought them into a likeness of my own state. I stood there while longing wrote lines with the pens of my tears on the effaced traces of my heart. I questioned the ruins about Abla and a raven answered me — afflicted as I am with mad wandering love. It mourns a companion and when it complains, it complains with sobbing not with the speech of a tongue. It laments from excess of hidden anguish, so I answered it with the sighing of a heart forever trembling. O raven of separation, if you were my companion we would have traversed God's lands in our wandering, hoping to find a messenger from Abla's direction, in whatever land, in whatever place.
